title: Wine Glossary
subtitle: Definitions for wine terminology from A to Z

terms.short_definition: A chemical compound formed during fermentation and oxidation.
terms.full_definition: Acetaldehyde is an intermediate compound in fermentation, normally converted to ethanol by yeast. When present in excess due to oxidation or incomplete fermentation, it imparts bruised apple, sherry-like, or nutty aromas. It's intentionally cultivated in Fino and Manzanilla sherries but considered a fault in most table wines.

terms.short_definition: The acid responsible for vinegar character in wine.
terms.full_definition: Acetic acid is produced by acetobacter bacteria when wine is exposed to oxygen. All wines contain trace amounts, but excessive levels (above 0.7-1.2 g/L depending on style) create vinegar-like sourness and a pungent, nail polish remover smell. It's a component of volatile acidity.

terms.short_definition: The tart, sharp quality in wine from natural grape acids.
terms.full_definition: Acidity refers to the presence of organic acids (primarily tartaric, malic, and citric) that give wine its crisp, fresh, tart character. Proper acidity is essential for balance, aging potential, food pairing, and microbial stability. Wines from cooler climates typically have higher acidity. Measured as total acidity (TA) in grams per liter or as pH.

terms.short_definition: Portuguese term for winery or wine cellar.
terms.full_definition: Adega is the Portuguese word for a winery, wine cellar, or wine-producing establishment. It appears in many Portuguese wine labels and producer names. The Spanish equivalent is bodega.

terms.short_definition: Exposing wine to air to soften tannins and release aromas.
terms.full_definition: Aeration introduces oxygen to wine, either through decanting, swirling, or specialized aerator devices. This can help young, tannic wines soften and open up, allowing volatile compounds to dissipate and aromatic compounds to express. Over-aeration can flatten older wines or cause premature oxidation.

terms.short_definition: The maturation of wine over time in barrel, tank, or bottle.
terms.full_definition: Aging allows wine to develop complexity through slow chemical reactions. Barrel aging adds oak flavors and allows micro-oxygenation. Bottle aging develops tertiary characteristics like leather, earth, and dried fruit. Not all wines benefit from extended aging; most are made for immediate consumption.

terms.short_definition: Ethanol produced during fermentation, expressed as percentage by volume.
terms.full_definition: Alcohol (ethanol) is produced when yeast converts grape sugars during fermentation. Wine typically ranges from 5.5% ABV (Moscato d'Asti) to 16%+ (Amarone, fortified wines). Alcohol contributes body, warmth, and sweetness perception. High alcohol can create a hot, burning sensation if unbalanced.

terms.short_definition: A system where wineries distribute limited wines to approved buyers.
terms.full_definition: Allocation is a distribution method used by high-demand wineries to control who can purchase their limited-production wines. Customers join mailing lists, build purchase history, and may wait years for allocation offers. Common with cult California Cabernets, top Burgundy, and prestige Champagne.

terms.short_definition: French wine region known for aromatic white varieties.
terms.full_definition: Alsace is a northeastern French region along the Rhine, producing predominantly white wines from Riesling, Gewurztraminer, Pinot Gris, and Muscat. Protected by the Vosges Mountains, it has a dry, sunny climate. Wines are typically dry and varietally labeled, unlike most French appellations. Grand Cru sites exist for top vineyards.

terms.short_definition: The scientific study and identification of grape varieties.
terms.full_definition: Ampelography is the botanical science of identifying and classifying grape vines based on leaf shape, cluster form, berry characteristics, and growth habits. Modern ampelography incorporates DNA analysis to trace grape ancestry and identify synonyms. It has revealed many surprising relationships and corrected historical misidentifications.

terms.short_definition: Pigment compounds in grape skins responsible for red wine color.
terms.full_definition: Anthocyanins are water-soluble pigments found in the skins of red and black grapes. They give red wine its color, ranging from purple-blue in young wines to brick-red in aged wines. During aging, anthocyanins polymerize with tannins, creating stable color compounds while softening the wine's texture.

terms.short_definition: Appellation d'Origine Controlee - French wine classification system.
terms.full_definition: AOC (now AOP under EU rules) is France's quality wine designation system, regulating geographic boundaries, permitted grape varieties, maximum yields, minimum alcohol, and winemaking practices. Established in 1935, it became the model for wine laws worldwide. Not a guarantee of quality, but of authenticity and typicity.

terms.short_definition: A legally defined wine-growing region.
terms.full_definition: An appellation is a geographically defined area where grapes are grown, governed by regulations that may specify permitted grape varieties, yields, winemaking practices, and labeling requirements. Examples include AOC (France), DOC/DOCG (Italy), DO (Spain), and AVA (USA). The term derives from the French word for 'name.'

terms.short_definition: Scents derived from the grape variety itself.
terms.full_definition: Aroma refers specifically to scents originating from the grape variety (primary aromas), as opposed to bouquet (secondary and tertiary aromas from fermentation and aging). Common varietal aromas include citrus in Riesling, black currant in Cabernet Sauvignon, and rose petals in Gewurztraminer.

terms.short_definition: The French term for blending wines from different lots.
terms.full_definition: Assemblage is the art of blending wines from different grape varieties, vineyard parcels, or vintages to create a final cuvee. It's particularly important in Champagne, Bordeaux, and Rhone production. The winemaker tastes individual lots and combines them to achieve balance, complexity, and house style.

terms.short_definition: The drying, puckering sensation from tannins.
terms.full_definition: Astringency is a tactile sensation, not a taste, caused by tannins binding with proteins in saliva. It creates a drying, gripping feeling on gums, tongue, and inner cheeks. Young red wines and heavily oaked whites often show high astringency, which typically softens with age or food pairing.

terms.short_definition: American Viticultural Area - US wine region designation.
terms.full_definition: AVA (American Viticultural Area) is the US appellation system, defining geographic wine regions based on climate, soil, and other distinguishing features. Unlike European systems, AVAs don't regulate grape varieties or winemaking. At least 85% of grapes must come from the named AVA. There are over 270 AVAs across the country.

terms.short_definition: Harmony between wine components: fruit, acid, tannin, alcohol.
terms.full_definition: Balance describes a wine where no single element dominates. Acidity, sweetness, tannin, alcohol, and fruit intensity should complement each other. A balanced wine feels harmonious and complete. Balance can exist at any quality level and is often considered the most important criterion in wine evaluation.

terms.short_definition: Wooden container used for fermenting and aging wine.
terms.full_definition: Barrels are typically made from oak and used to ferment and/or age wine. A standard Bordeaux barrique holds 225 liters; a Burgundy piece holds 228 liters. New barrels contribute oak flavors (vanilla, spice, toast) and allow slow oxygen exchange. Older barrels are more neutral, used for texture without added flavor.

terms.short_definition: A 225-liter oak barrel, the Bordeaux standard.
terms.full_definition: The barrique is a 225-liter (59-gallon) oak barrel originating from Bordeaux. This size became international standard due to Bordeaux's historical trade dominance. The relatively small size provides good surface-to-wine ratio for oak extraction and micro-oxygenation. Burgundy uses slightly larger 228-liter pieces.

terms.short_definition: Stirring wine on its lees to add texture and complexity.
terms.full_definition: Batonnage (lees stirring) involves agitating settled yeast cells back into wine using a stick or mechanical device. This releases mannoproteins and other compounds, adding body, creaminess, and complexity. Common in white Burgundy and quality Chardonnay production. Excessive batonnage can create heavy, oxidized wines.

terms.short_definition: Holistic farming approach following Rudolf Steiner's principles.
terms.full_definition: Biodynamic viticulture follows Austrian philosopher Rudolf Steiner's 1924 teachings, treating the vineyard as a self-sustaining ecosystem. It includes organic practices plus specific preparations (horn manure, horn silica), lunar/cosmic timing for vineyard work, and philosophical elements. Certified by Demeter. Many prestigious estates practice biodynamics.

terms.short_definition: A sharp, acidic sensation in wine.
terms.full_definition: Bite refers to a pronounced acidic edge in wine that creates a sharp, crisp sensation on the palate. It can be a positive characteristic in fresh whites and sparkling wines, or negative when excessive and out of balance. Similar to but stronger than 'crisp' or 'bright.'

terms.short_definition: White wine made exclusively from white grapes, typically Chardonnay.
terms.full_definition: Blanc de Blancs ('white from whites') refers to sparkling wine made entirely from white grape varieties. In Champagne, this means 100% Chardonnay, producing wines known for elegance, minerality, and citrus character. The style tends to be lighter and more age-worthy than blended Champagnes.

terms.short_definition: White wine made from red grapes, typically Pinot Noir.
terms.full_definition: Blanc de Noirs ('white from blacks') is white or pale pink sparkling wine made from red grape varieties, usually Pinot Noir and/or Pinot Meunier in Champagne. The juice is quickly pressed off the skins to minimize color extraction. These wines tend to be fuller-bodied with red fruit notes.

terms.short_definition: Wine made from multiple grape varieties or vineyard sources.
terms.full_definition: A blend combines wines from different grape varieties, vineyard sites, or vintages. Blending allows winemakers to achieve complexity and balance unattainable from single sources. Famous blending regions include Bordeaux (Cabernet/Merlot), Rhone (Grenache/Syrah/Mourvedre), and Champagne. Field blends are multiple varieties grown and vinified together.

terms.short_definition: The weight and fullness of wine on the palate.
terms.full_definition: Body describes how heavy or light a wine feels in the mouth, influenced by alcohol, extract, glycerol, and sugar content. Light-bodied wines (Muscadet, Riesling) feel like skim milk; medium-bodied (Burgundy, Chianti) like whole milk; full-bodied (Amarone, Napa Cabernet) like cream. Body doesn't indicate quality.

terms.short_definition: Prestigious French region known for Cabernet and Merlot blends.
terms.full_definition: Bordeaux is France's largest fine wine region, producing red blends (primarily Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, Cabernet Franc) and dry/sweet whites (Sauvignon Blanc, Semillon). The Left Bank emphasizes Cabernet; the Right Bank, Merlot. The 1855 Classification established its famous hierarchy. Bordeaux defined much of modern wine culture.

terms.short_definition: Noble rot fungus that concentrates sugars for sweet wines.
terms.full_definition: Botrytis cinerea ('noble rot') is a beneficial fungus that dehydrates grapes, concentrating sugars and adding honey, apricot, and ginger flavors. Essential for Sauternes, Tokaji, and Trockenbeerenauslese. Requires specific conditions: morning fog, afternoon sun. Grey rot (same fungus in wrong conditions) destroys crops.

terms.short_definition: Temporary muted condition after bottling or transport.
terms.full_definition: Bottle shock is a temporary condition where wine tastes flat, disjointed, or muted, typically occurring right after bottling or vigorous transport. The dissolved oxygen from bottling needs time to integrate. Wine usually recovers within days to weeks of rest. Also called 'bottle sickness.'

terms.short_definition: Complex aromas developed through fermentation and aging.
terms.full_definition: Bouquet refers to secondary and tertiary aromas that develop from fermentation and aging, as distinct from primary varietal aromas. These include yeast-derived scents (bread, brioche), oak contributions (vanilla, toast), and bottle age characteristics (leather, earth, mushroom). A developed bouquet indicates mature wine.

terms.short_definition: Allowing wine to be exposed to air before serving.
terms.full_definition: Breathing exposes wine to oxygen, either by uncorking and waiting, or more effectively through decanting. This can help young wines soften and aromatic compounds develop. However, simply uncorking provides minimal surface area; actual decanting is more effective. Not all wines benefit from breathing.

terms.short_definition: Shortened term for Brettanomyces, a spoilage yeast.
terms.full_definition: Brett is winemaker shorthand for Brettanomyces, a yeast that produces barnyard, horse blanket, Band-Aid, and smoky aromas. At low levels, some consider it adds complexity (traditional Rhone, older Bordeaux); at high levels, it's a fault. It thrives in barrel environments and is extremely difficult to eradicate.

terms.short_definition: A spoilage yeast producing barnyard and medicinal aromas.
terms.full_definition: Brettanomyces is a genus of yeast that produces 4-ethylphenol and 4-ethylguaiacol, compounds smelling of barnyard, horse sweat, Band-Aid, and smoke. It ferments residual sugars in finished wine and lives in wood barrels. Controversial: some regions traditionally accept low levels as terroir character; modern winemaking typically strives for brett-free wines.

terms.short_definition: Wine with vibrant, fresh acidity.
terms.full_definition: Bright describes wines with lively, refreshing acidity that makes the wine feel vibrant and energetic. It's generally a positive descriptor, suggesting freshness and vitality. Bright wines stimulate the palate and pair well with food. Opposite of dull, flat, or flabby.

terms.short_definition: Measurement of sugar content in grape juice.
terms.full_definition: Brix is a scale measuring dissolved sugar content in grape must, used to determine ripeness and potential alcohol. One degree Brix equals 1 gram sugar per 100 grams solution. Most table wine grapes are harvested between 22-26 Brix. Each Brix degree converts to roughly 0.55-0.60% alcohol.

terms.short_definition: Dry sparkling wine with minimal residual sugar.
terms.full_definition: Brut is a sweetness level for sparkling wine, indicating less than 12 grams per liter of residual sugar. It's the most common Champagne style. Drier styles include Extra Brut (0-6 g/L) and Brut Nature/Zero (0-3 g/L). Despite being called 'dry,' brut wines have enough sugar to balance high acidity.

terms.short_definition: French region producing prestigious Pinot Noir and Chardonnay.
terms.full_definition: Burgundy (Bourgogne) is eastern France's premier region for Pinot Noir and Chardonnay. Its complex classification system includes Regional, Village, Premier Cru, and Grand Cru levels. The concept of terroir is central: tiny vineyard differences create dramatically different wines. Notoriously expensive and allocation-limited at top levels.

terms.short_definition: Rich, creamy character often from malolactic fermentation.
terms.full_definition: Buttery describes a creamy, rich texture and flavor in wine, typically white wines that have undergone malolactic fermentation. Diacetyl, a byproduct of ML, creates actual butter-like aromas. Associated with oaked Chardonnay styles. Can be a positive or negative descriptor depending on preference and balance.

terms.short_definition: Techniques to control vine leaf and shoot growth.
terms.full_definition: Canopy management encompasses all techniques to shape vine leaf and shoot growth for optimal grape quality. This includes trellising systems (VSP, Geneva Double Curtain), leaf pulling, shoot thinning, hedging, and cluster thinning. Goals include sunlight exposure, air circulation, disease prevention, and balanced ripening.

terms.short_definition: The foil or plastic covering over a wine bottle's cork.
terms.full_definition: The capsule is the protective covering over a wine bottle's closure, originally made of lead (now banned for wine), currently tin, aluminum, or plastic. It protects the cork from damage and pests, and serves as a tamper indicator. Some natural wine producers skip capsules entirely.

terms.short_definition: Whole-grape fermentation in CO2, producing fruity wines.
terms.full_definition: Carbonic maceration ferments whole, uncrushed grapes in a carbon dioxide-rich environment. Intracellular fermentation within the berry creates distinctive fruity, candy-like flavors with low tannin. It's the traditional Beaujolais method, producing the famous Beaujolais Nouveau style. Semi-carbonic maceration partially crushes grapes.

terms.short_definition: Spanish sparkling wine made by traditional method.
terms.full_definition: Cava is Spain's traditional method sparkling wine, primarily from Catalonia using Macabeo, Xarel-lo, and Parellada grapes. It must undergo secondary fermentation in bottle and minimum 9 months aging on lees (15 for Reserva, 30 for Gran Reserva). Offers good value compared to Champagne.

terms.short_definition: Storing wine under proper conditions for aging.
terms.full_definition: Cellaring means storing wine in conditions suitable for aging: 55F/13C temperature, 70% humidity, darkness, stillness, and horizontal positioning for corked bottles. Not all wines improve with cellaring; most are meant for immediate consumption. Wines with high acidity, tannin, and concentration age best.

terms.short_definition: French term for an above-ground wine storage building.
terms.full_definition: A chai is a Bordeaux term for an above-ground building where wine is stored in barrels, as opposed to underground caves. The high water table in much of Bordeaux makes underground cellars impractical. The chai master (maitre de chai) oversees barrel aging and winemaking.

terms.short_definition: Sparkling wine from the Champagne region of France.
terms.full_definition: Champagne is sparkling wine exclusively from the Champagne region of northeastern France, made by the traditional method (methode champenoise). Primary grapes are Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, and Pinot Meunier. The cool climate, chalky soils, and strict regulations create distinctive wines that define the sparkling wine category worldwide.

terms.short_definition: Adding sugar to must to increase alcohol level.
terms.full_definition: Chaptalization is adding sugar to grape must before fermentation to raise potential alcohol in cool vintages when grapes don't fully ripen. Named after Napoleon's minister Jean-Antoine Chaptal. Legal in cool regions (Burgundy, Germany, Oregon) but banned in warm regions (California, Southern France, Italy). The sugar ferments completely; it doesn't sweeten the wine.

terms.short_definition: Tank fermentation method for sparkling wine.
terms.full_definition: The Charmat (or tank) method conducts secondary fermentation in large pressurized tanks rather than individual bottles. It's faster and cheaper than traditional method, preserving fresh fruit character. Used for Prosecco, Lambrusco, and many affordable sparkling wines. The wine spends less time on lees, producing simpler, fruitier bubbles.

terms.short_definition: French wine estate, especially in Bordeaux.
terms.full_definition: Chateau literally means 'castle' but in wine refers to a wine-producing estate, particularly in Bordeaux. The term implies grapes are grown and wine made at the property, though actual castles may or may not exist. Famous chateaux include Lafite, Latour, Margaux, and Petrus.

terms.short_definition: Dense, substantial texture with firm tannins.
terms.full_definition: Chewy describes wines with substantial texture that seem to require actual chewing - dense, full-bodied wines with firm but ripe tannins. It suggests weight and substance, typically in young red wines that need time or food. Generally a positive descriptor indicating concentration and aging potential.

terms.short_definition: Tuscan red wine, primarily Sangiovese.
terms.full_definition: Chianti is a red wine from Tuscany, Italy, based on Sangiovese grapes. Chianti Classico is the historic heart; other zones include Chianti Rufina and Colli Senesi. Modern Chianti ranges from simple, fresh versions to serious, age-worthy wines. The straw-covered flask (fiasco) is now rare except in tourist markets.

terms.short_definition: Removing suspended particles from wine.
terms.full_definition: Clarification removes proteins, dead yeast, grape particles, and other matter that cause cloudiness. Methods include natural settling (racking), fining (adding agents that bind with particles), and filtration. Over-clarification can strip color, flavor, and texture. Some wines are bottled unfined and unfiltered.

terms.short_definition: Official ranking system for wines or wine estates.
terms.full_definition: A classification is an official ranking of wines or wine estates based on quality, price, or reputation. Famous examples include Bordeaux's 1855 Classification, Burgundy's Grand Cru/Premier Cru system, and Germany's VDP rankings. Classifications may be legally binding or honorary, and some haven't been updated in over a century.

terms.short_definition: A named vineyard parcel in Burgundy.
terms.full_definition: Climat is the Burgundian term for a specifically named and delimited vineyard plot, recognized for its unique terroir characteristics. Burgundy has over 1,200 climats, each producing wines of distinct character. The climats of Burgundy were granted UNESCO World Heritage status in 2015.

terms.short_definition: A vine variety propagated from a single parent plant.
terms.full_definition: A clone is a vine propagated vegetatively from a single mother vine, selected for specific characteristics like disease resistance, yield, cluster size, or flavor profile. Different clones of the same variety can produce notably different wines. Famous examples include Dijon clones of Pinot Noir and Chardonnay.

terms.short_definition: A walled vineyard, traditionally in Burgundy.
terms.full_definition: Clos (plural: clos) is a French term for a walled vineyard. The walls historically protected vines and helped ripen grapes through reflected heat. Many famous Burgundy vineyards are clos: Clos de Vougeot, Clos de Tart, Clos des Lambrays. Some New World wineries use 'clos' as a marketing term.

terms.short_definition: The seal used to close a wine bottle.
terms.full_definition: Closure refers to what seals a wine bottle: natural cork, technical cork, synthetic cork, screwcap (Stelvin), glass stopper (Vino-Lok), or crown cap. Each has different oxygen transmission rates affecting aging. The closure debate - particularly cork vs. screwcap - remains contentious in the wine industry.

terms.short_definition: Pre-fermentation maceration at low temperature.
terms.full_definition: Cold soak (or cold maceration) is a technique where crushed red grapes are held at low temperatures (5-10C) for several days before fermentation begins. This extracts color and fruit character without tannin (alcohol is needed for tannin extraction). It can enhance fruitiness and color in varieties like Pinot Noir.

terms.short_definition: Multiple layers of aromas, flavors, and textures.
terms.full_definition: Complexity describes wines with many different aromas, flavors, and textural elements that reveal themselves over time. Complex wines evolve in the glass and show new facets with each sip. It's generally a mark of quality and often develops with age. The opposite is simple or one-dimensional.

terms.short_definition: Intense, focused flavors with density.
terms.full_definition: Concentrated wines have intense, focused flavors and dense texture, often from low yields, old vines, or specific winemaking techniques. Concentration implies substance and extract. It's generally positive but can become excessive if unbalanced. Different from simply being high in alcohol or fruit.

terms.short_definition: The craft of barrel-making, or a barrel-making facility.
terms.full_definition: Cooperage refers to both the craft of making wooden barrels and the facility where they're made. Famous cooperages include Seguin Moreau, Taransaud, and Francois Freres. Each cooperage has distinctive house styles based on wood sourcing, seasoning, and toasting. Barrel choice significantly impacts wine flavor.

terms.short_definition: A permanent horizontal arm of a grapevine.
terms.full_definition: A cordon is a permanent horizontal extension of the vine trunk, trained along a wire. In cordon training, fruiting spurs grow from this permanent arm. Bilateral cordon has arms extending in both directions. Cordon training is efficient and consistent but may reduce wine complexity compared to cane pruning.

terms.short_definition: Traditional wine closure made from bark of cork oak.
terms.full_definition: Cork closures are made from the bark of Quercus suber (cork oak), primarily from Portugal and Spain. Cork allows micro-oxygenation for aging while maintaining seal. Natural cork risks TCA contamination (cork taint). Alternatives include technical corks (agglomerated or multi-piece) with lower TCA risk.

terms.short_definition: Musty, wet cardboard smell caused by TCA contamination.
terms.full_definition: Cork taint is a fault caused primarily by 2,4,6-trichloroanisole (TCA), producing musty, wet cardboard, moldy basement aromas and muting fruit. TCA forms when natural fungi in cork interact with chlorine-based sanitizers. It affects 2-5% of corked wines. Even below detection threshold, TCA can suppress fruitiness.

terms.short_definition: Wine contaminated with TCA, showing musty aromas.
terms.full_definition: A corked wine has been contaminated with TCA (trichloroanisole), displaying musty, damp cardboard, or moldy newspaper aromas. The fruit is muted or absent. Severity ranges from subtle suppression to obvious contamination. It's estimated 2-5% of naturally corked wines are affected to some degree.

terms.short_definition: The 'Golden Slope' of Burgundy, home to Grand Crus.
terms.full_definition: The Cote d'Or ('Golden Slope') is Burgundy's prestigious heart, divided into Cote de Nuits (primarily Pinot Noir) in the north and Cote de Beaune (both varieties) in the south. This limestone escarpment contains most of Burgundy's Grand Cru and Premier Cru vineyards. Wines from here command the highest prices.

terms.short_definition: French sparkling wine made outside Champagne by traditional method.
terms.full_definition: Cremant is the term for French traditional method sparkling wines from regions other than Champagne. Major examples include Cremant d'Alsace, Cremant de Bourgogne, Cremant de Loire, and Cremant de Limoux. They often offer excellent value as alternatives to Champagne, using regional grape varieties.

terms.short_definition: Refreshing acidity in wine.
terms.full_definition: Crisp describes wines with bright, refreshing acidity that creates a clean, fresh sensation. It's typically applied to white wines and suggests firm acidity without being sour or sharp. Crisp wines are food-friendly and palate-cleansing. The opposite of soft, flabby, or flat.

terms.short_definition: French term for a vineyard or wine of recognized quality.
terms.full_definition: Cru means 'growth' in French and refers to a vineyard site of recognized quality. Usage varies by region: in Burgundy, Grand Cru and Premier Cru are legal classifications; in Bordeaux, cru classe means classified growth; in Beaujolais, cru refers to the ten named villages. Cru implies terroir-driven distinction.

terms.short_definition: The harvest and initial processing of grapes.
terms.full_definition: Crush refers to both the grape harvest period and the mechanical process of breaking grape skins to release juice. During crush, wineries are at peak activity receiving, sorting, crushing, and beginning fermentation. 'Crush pad' is the area where incoming grapes are processed.

terms.short_definition: A specific blend or batch of wine.
terms.full_definition: Cuvee literally means 'tank' or 'vat' but refers to a specific blend or batch of wine. In Champagne, it can mean the first (best) press juice or a particular house blend. Elsewhere, 'cuvee' often denotes a special selection or proprietary blend. Usage is unregulated and varies widely.

terms.short_definition: Pouring wine into another vessel for aeration or sediment removal.
terms.full_definition: Decanting transfers wine from bottle to another container (decanter). For young wines, it exposes wine to oxygen, softening tannins and releasing aromas. For old wines, it separates clear wine from sediment. Double decanting returns wine to the rinsed bottle for service.

terms.short_definition: Rack-and-return technique for gentle extraction.
terms.full_definition: Delestage (rack-and-return) is a red winemaking technique where fermenting juice is drained from the tank, the cap settles, then the juice is pumped back over. This provides gentle extraction, aerates the must, and can help prevent reduction. It extracts less harsh tannin than traditional punch-down or pump-over.

terms.short_definition: Medium-sweet sparkling wine.
terms.full_definition: Demi-sec ('half-dry') is a sweetness level for sparkling wine containing 32-50 grams per liter residual sugar. Despite the name, it's noticeably sweet. Demi-sec Champagnes pair well with desserts, particularly fruit-based ones. The Loire Valley uses demi-sec for medium-sweet still wines like Vouvray.

terms.short_definition: In tasting, the concentration of a wine; in viticulture, vine spacing.
terms.full_definition: Density has two wine meanings: in tasting, it describes wines with concentrated, compact flavors and texture. In viticulture, plant density refers to vine spacing - high-density planting (10,000+ vines/hectare in Burgundy) increases root competition, reduces vigor, and can improve quality. Low-density (1,000-2,000/hectare) is common in warm, dry regions.

terms.short_definition: Layers of flavor that reveal themselves over time.
terms.full_definition: Depth describes wines with multiple layers of flavor that unfold as you taste. Deep wines reveal new characteristics with each sip and continue evolving in the glass. It suggests substance, complexity, and quality. Shallow or superficial wines lack depth.

terms.short_definition: Removing sediment from sparkling wine after secondary fermentation.
terms.full_definition: Disgorgement (degorgement) removes the yeast sediment from sparkling wine after secondary fermentation and aging on lees. The bottle neck is frozen, the cap removed, and pressure expels the frozen plug of sediment. Dosage is added, and a permanent cork inserted. Some wines are disgorged to order for maximum freshness.

terms.short_definition: The difference between day and night temperatures.
terms.full_definition: Diurnal range (or diurnal shift) is the temperature difference between daytime highs and nighttime lows. Large diurnal ranges (common in high altitude or continental climates) help grapes develop flavor complexity while retaining acidity, as cool nights slow respiration and preserve acids. Regions like Mendoza and Walla Walla are noted for this.

terms.short_definition: Italian and Portuguese quality wine designation.
terms.full_definition: DOC (Denominazione di Origine Controllata in Italy; Denominacao de Origem Controlada in Portugal) is a quality wine classification similar to French AOC. It specifies geographic origin, permitted grape varieties, yields, aging requirements, and production methods. Italy's higher DOCG adds government tasting approval.

terms.short_definition: French term for a wine estate, especially in Burgundy.
terms.full_definition: Domaine is the French term for a wine estate where grapes are grown, wine is made, and typically bottled on-site. Most common in Burgundy, where 'mis en bouteille au domaine' (estate bottled) indicates quality. Unlike Bordeaux's chateau, a domaine doesn't imply a grand building.

terms.short_definition: Sugar solution added to sparkling wine after disgorgement.
terms.full_definition: Dosage is the addition of liqueur d'expedition (wine and sugar solution) to sparkling wine after disgorgement, determining final sweetness level. Dosage ranges from zero (Brut Nature) to substantial (Doux). The dosage balances the wine's high natural acidity and can include reserve wines for complexity.

terms.short_definition: Wine with little or no residual sugar.
terms.full_definition: Dry wine has fermented nearly all sugar to alcohol, typically less than 4 g/L residual sugar. 'Dry' is also used colloquially to describe tannic wines that create a drying mouthfeel. Many wines perceived as fruity are technically dry; fruit flavor doesn't equal sweetness.

terms.short_definition: Aromas and flavors suggesting soil, mushroom, or minerals.
terms.full_definition: Earthy describes wines with aromas and flavors reminiscent of soil, forest floor, mushrooms, truffles, wet leaves, or minerals. It's often a positive descriptor suggesting terroir expression, particularly in aged Burgundy, Barolo, and traditional European wines. Excessive earthiness can be a fault.

terms.short_definition: German ice wine made from grapes frozen on the vine.
terms.full_definition: Eiswein (ice wine) is made from grapes naturally frozen on the vine, harvested and pressed while frozen. Water remains as ice, yielding intensely concentrated, sweet juice. Authentic Eiswein requires temperatures below -7C and is risky to produce. Germany, Austria, and Canada are primary producers.

terms.short_definition: The period of wine maturation between fermentation and bottling.
terms.full_definition: Elevage (literally 'raising' or 'upbringing') encompasses all cellar work between fermentation and bottling: aging in barrel or tank, racking, fining, blending. Good elevage allows wine to develop complexity and stability. The winemaker's choices during elevage significantly shape the final wine.

terms.short_definition: Buying wine as futures before bottling.
terms.full_definition: En primeur (or wine futures) is purchasing wine before it's bottled, typically during barrel aging. The Bordeaux en primeur system offers wines in spring following harvest, for delivery 18-24 months later. Buyers gamble on quality assessments and future prices. Other regions including Burgundy have adopted similar systems.

terms.short_definition: The science and study of winemaking.
terms.full_definition: Enology (or oenology) is the science of winemaking, encompassing fermentation, chemistry, microbiology, and all cellar practices. An enologist studies or practices this science professionally. Major enology programs exist at UC Davis, Bordeaux, Geisenheim, and Adelaide.

terms.short_definition: Aromatic compounds formed during fermentation.
terms.full_definition: Esters are volatile compounds formed when acids react with alcohols during fermentation. They contribute fruity and floral aromas - ethyl acetate (nail polish/solvent at high levels), isoamyl acetate (banana), and many others. Ester production depends on yeast strain, fermentation temperature, and nutrient availability.

terms.short_definition: Wine made and bottled by the grape grower.
terms.full_definition: Estate bottled (or estate grown) indicates the winery grew the grapes, made the wine, and bottled it, all in the same appellation. In the US, both vineyard and winery must be in the same AVA. It suggests quality control from vine to bottle, though isn't itself a quality guarantee.

terms.short_definition: Compound causing nail polish or solvent aromas.
terms.full_definition: Ethyl acetate is an ester that smells of nail polish remover or solvent. All wines contain some, but excessive amounts (above 150 mg/L) indicate a fault, often from acetobacter or oxidation. At moderate levels, it can contribute fruity character. It's a component of volatile acidity.

terms.short_definition: The non-volatile soluble solids in wine.
terms.full_definition: Extract refers to everything in wine except water, alcohol, and acids - sugars, phenolics, glycerol, minerals, and other compounds that contribute to body, texture, and flavor intensity. High extract wines feel dense and substantial. Extract is influenced by yield, ripeness, and winemaking technique.

terms.short_definition: The conversion of sugar to alcohol by yeast.
terms.full_definition: Fermentation is the metabolic process where yeast converts grape sugars (glucose and fructose) into ethanol and carbon dioxide. Primary (alcoholic) fermentation creates wine from juice. Secondary fermentation can refer to malolactic conversion or, in sparkling wine, the bubble-creating fermentation in bottle or tank.

terms.short_definition: Multiple grape varieties planted and harvested together.
terms.full_definition: A field blend contains multiple grape varieties planted in the same vineyard, harvested together, and co-fermented. Traditional in old vineyards before varietal planting became standard. Field blends can produce uniquely integrated wines, as varieties ripen and ferment together. Common in old-vine Zinfandel and Portuguese wines.

terms.short_definition: Removing particles from wine by passing through a filter.
terms.full_definition: Filtration clarifies wine and provides microbial stability by passing wine through filter media. Options include depth filtration, membrane filtration, and crossflow filtration, at various pore sizes. Aggressive filtration can strip color and flavor; some winemakers bottle unfiltered for maximum complexity and texture.

terms.short_definition: The taste and sensations that linger after swallowing.
terms.full_definition: The finish (or aftertaste) is what remains on the palate after swallowing or spitting wine. A long finish - flavors persisting for 30+ seconds - generally indicates quality. The finish may echo the primary flavors or introduce new elements. Short, bitter, or hot finishes are considered faults.

terms.short_definition: Clarifying wine by adding substances that bind with particles.
terms.full_definition: Fining uses additives that bind with suspended particles and fall to the bottom, clarifying wine. Traditional agents include egg whites (for tannin reduction), bentonite (proteins), isinglass (fish bladder), casein (milk protein), and gelatin. Vegan alternatives exist. Some winemakers skip fining for fuller texture.

terms.short_definition: The top-ranked estates in Bordeaux's 1855 Classification.
terms.full_definition: First Growth (Premier Cru) refers to the five top-ranked estates in the 1855 Bordeaux Classification: Lafite Rothschild, Latour, Margaux, Haut-Brion, and Mouton Rothschild (elevated in 1973). These wines command the highest prices and are considered among the world's greatest. 'Super Seconds' approach First Growth quality.

terms.short_definition: Wine lacking sufficient acidity, tasting soft and dull.
terms.full_definition: Flabby describes wine with insufficient acidity, resulting in a flat, soft, lifeless character. The wine lacks freshness and structure, feeling heavy and unfocused. Flabbiness can result from overripe grapes, hot climates, or excessive malolactic fermentation. The opposite of crisp or bright.

terms.short_definition: Wine lacking acidity, or sparkling wine that has lost bubbles.
terms.full_definition: Flat has two meanings: for still wines, it indicates insufficient acidity, making wine dull and lifeless (similar to flabby). For sparkling wines, flat means the wine has lost its carbon dioxide, becoming still. Both usages are negative descriptors.

terms.short_definition: Film-forming yeast that creates the distinctive character of Fino sherry.
terms.full_definition: Flor is a layer of indigenous yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) that forms on the surface of certain sherries, protecting from oxidation while adding distinctive tangy, salty, bread-dough character. It requires specific alcohol levels (15-15.5%) and humidity to thrive. Fino, Manzanilla, and biological-aged sherries are defined by flor.

terms.short_definition: Adding grape spirit to wine to increase alcohol.
terms.full_definition: Fortification adds neutral grape spirit (brandy) to wine, raising alcohol content. When added during fermentation, it kills yeast and preserves sweetness (Port, most Madeira). When added after fermentation, it produces dry wines (Sherry, Vin Doux Naturel). Fortified wines typically reach 15-22% ABV.

terms.short_definition: Juice or wine that drains naturally without pressing.
terms.full_definition: Free run is juice that drains from grapes by gravity alone, without pressing. For white wines, free run juice is collected as grapes are loaded into the press. For reds, it's the wine that drains from the tank after fermentation. Free run is typically lighter, more elegant, and higher quality than press wine.

terms.short_definition: Oak from French forests, prized for fine-grained character.
terms.full_definition: French oak (Quercus petraea, Quercus robur) from forests like Tronçais, Nevers, Allier, and Vosges is considered the premium barrel material. Its tight grain releases flavors gradually, contributing subtle spice, vanilla, and toast notes. More expensive than American oak, it's favored for Burgundy, Bordeaux, and premium wines worldwide.

terms.short_definition: Wine with bright acidity and lively fruit character.
terms.full_definition: Fresh describes wines with vibrant acidity and clean, lively fruit character that make the wine feel energetic and thirst-quenching. It's typically positive, especially for whites and roses meant for immediate consumption. Fresh wines contrast with oxidized, tired, or overly developed wines.

terms.short_definition: Wine where fruit flavors dominate the profile.
terms.full_definition: Fruit forward describes wines where ripe fruit character is the primary impression, rather than earth, oak, or structure. Often associated with New World styles and warm climates. Neither inherently positive nor negative - it describes a style. The opposite is earthy, savory, or structured.

terms.short_definition: Wine with substantial weight and richness on the palate.
terms.full_definition: Full-bodied wines have significant weight, richness, and density on the palate, often from high alcohol, ripe fruit, and substantial extract. Examples include Napa Cabernet, Barossa Shiraz, and Amarone. Full body isn't inherently better - it's a style suited to certain foods and occasions.

terms.short_definition: The red grape of Beaujolais, producing light, fruity wines.
terms.full_definition: Gamay (Gamay Noir a Jus Blanc) is the signature grape of Beaujolais, France, producing light to medium-bodied red wines with bright acidity and red fruit character. Often made by carbonic maceration for fresh, fruity style. The ten Beaujolais crus can produce serious, age-worthy wines. Also grown in Loire Valley and Switzerland.

terms.short_definition: Wild herb aromas associated with Mediterranean wines.
terms.full_definition: Garrigue refers to the aromatic scrubland of southern France - wild herbs like thyme, rosemary, lavender, and sage. As a tasting term, it describes these herbal, resinous aromas in wines from Provence, Rhone, and other Mediterranean regions. It's considered a marker of terroir in these areas.

terms.short_definition: The vessel for serving wine, affecting aroma and perception.
terms.full_definition: Wine glass design affects how aromas are concentrated and delivered to the nose. Larger bowls allow more aeration; narrower rims concentrate aromas. Different shapes suit different wines: wide Burgundy bowls for Pinot Noir, taller Bordeaux glasses for Cabernet, narrow flutes for Champagne. Quality crystal is thinner at the rim.

terms.short_definition: A byproduct of fermentation contributing to wine texture.
terms.full_definition: Glycerol (glycerine) is produced during fermentation, contributing to wine's viscosity, body, and perceived sweetness. Higher alcohol wines and botrytized wines have more glycerol. It creates the 'legs' or 'tears' that run down the glass, though this is primarily an alcohol effect. Glycerol has minimal taste impact.

terms.short_definition: Traditional bush vine training without trellising.
terms.full_definition: Gobelet (bush vine or head-trained) is an ancient vine training system where each vine stands as a free-standing bush without trellising. Common in hot, dry climates (Southern Rhone, Spain, old California) where the canopy shades fruit from intense sun. Labor-intensive but produces concentrated wines from low-yielding old vines.

terms.short_definition: Joining a fruiting vine variety to a resistant rootstock.
terms.full_definition: Grafting attaches a scion (fruiting variety) to a rootstock (resistant root system). This became essential after phylloxera devastated European vineyards in the late 1800s. American rootstocks resist the pest while allowing European varieties to grow. Nearly all vines worldwide are now grafted, except in isolated phylloxera-free regions.

terms.short_definition: The highest vineyard classification in Burgundy and Alsace.
terms.full_definition: Grand Cru ('great growth') is the top vineyard classification in Burgundy and Alsace. In Burgundy, 33 Grand Cru vineyards produce wines labeled only with the vineyard name, no village. In Alsace, 51 Grand Cru sites exist for specified grape varieties. The term is used differently in Champagne (village rating) and Bordeaux (estate ranking).

terms.short_definition: Removing unripe grape clusters to concentrate remaining fruit.
terms.full_definition: Green harvest (vendange verte) removes unripe clusters mid-summer to reduce yield and concentrate flavors in remaining fruit. It forces the vine to direct energy into fewer clusters, potentially improving ripeness and intensity. Common in premium red wine production. Timing is critical - too early or late reduces effectiveness.

terms.short_definition: Major red variety of Southern France and Spain.
terms.full_definition: Grenache (Garnacha in Spain) is a high-alcohol, fruity red grape thriving in hot, dry climates. It's the dominant variety in Chateauneuf-du-Pape and forms GSM blends with Syrah and Mourvedre. Grenache produces wines with red fruit, spice, and soft tannins but can oxidize easily. Also makes excellent rose and fortified wines.

terms.short_definition: The firm, mouth-drying sensation from tannins.
terms.full_definition: Grip describes the tactile sensation of tannins holding or gripping the palate. It's distinct from astringency in being firmer and more structured. A wine with good grip has substantial tannin that provides structure without being harsh. Common descriptor for young, age-worthy red wines.

terms.short_definition: Champagne made by the grape grower, not a large house.
terms.full_definition: Grower Champagne (Recoltant-Manipulant, marked 'RM' on label) is made by farmers who grow grapes and produce wine from their own vineyards, as opposed to large houses (negociant-manipulants) that buy grapes. Grower Champagnes often express specific terroir and offer value alternatives to famous brands.

terms.short_definition: Blend of Grenache, Syrah, and Mourvedre.
terms.full_definition: GSM is shorthand for blends of Grenache, Syrah (Shiraz), and Mourvedre - the classic Southern Rhone combination. Each grape contributes: Grenache brings fruit and alcohol, Syrah adds color and spice, Mourvedre provides structure and earthiness. The acronym is commonly used in Australia, where the blend is popular.

terms.short_definition: Vine training system using one or two canes.
terms.full_definition: Guyot is a cane-pruning system where one (single Guyot) or two (double Guyot) fruiting canes are retained each year, with spurs for next year's canes. Common in Burgundy, Bordeaux, and cool climates. It offers more flexibility than cordon training and is thought to produce more complex wines, though it requires more skilled labor.

terms.short_definition: A weather hazard that can destroy grape crops.
terms.full_definition: Hail is one of viticulture's most feared hazards - it can destroy a vintage in minutes, damaging leaves, shoots, and fruit. Hail netting provides protection but is expensive. Hail damage creates entry points for disease and can devastate yields. Some regions like Burgundy and Mendoza are particularly hail-prone.

terms.short_definition: The picking of grapes, typically in autumn.
terms.full_definition: Harvest (vendange in French, vendimia in Spanish) is the grape-picking season, usually August-October in Northern Hemisphere, February-April in Southern. Harvest timing is critical - too early yields unripe flavors, too late risks overripeness or rot. Harvest can be mechanical or by hand, with hand-harvesting preferred for premium wines.

terms.short_definition: A period of extreme heat that can damage vines and grapes.
terms.full_definition: Heat spikes are sudden, extreme temperature events that can shut down vine photosynthesis, sunburn grapes, cause dehydration, and accelerate ripening before flavor development completes. Climate change is increasing heat spike frequency. Vineyard practices like shade cloth and irrigation help mitigate damage.

terms.short_definition: Aromas of green herbs, grass, or vegetables.
terms.full_definition: Herbaceous describes green, vegetal aromas like bell pepper, grass, herbs, or asparagus. At moderate levels, it's varietal character for Sauvignon Blanc and Cabernet Franc. Excessive herbaceousness, especially in red wines, suggests underripe grapes or excessive canopy shading. Methoxypyrazines cause these aromas.

terms.short_definition: A 300-liter barrel common in Australia.
terms.full_definition: A hogshead is a barrel holding approximately 300 liters, larger than a standard barrique. Common in Australia, it provides less oak influence per volume of wine due to lower surface-to-volume ratio. The term comes from English measure, originally used for various commodities.

terms.short_definition: Comparing multiple wines from the same vintage.
terms.full_definition: A horizontal tasting compares wines from the same vintage but different producers or vineyards. This isolates vintage character while highlighting producer and site differences. For example, tasting six 2018 Napa Cabernets. The contrast is vertical tasting, which samples multiple vintages from one producer.

terms.short_definition: Excessive alcohol creating a burning sensation.
terms.full_definition: Hot describes wine where alcohol is out of balance, creating a burning, warming sensation in the throat that dominates other flavors. It's a fault indicating poor balance, often from overripe grapes or hot-climate growing. Well-balanced high-alcohol wines integrate the warmth without feeling hot.

terms.short_definition: A grape variety bred from two different species.
terms.full_definition: Hybrids are grape varieties created by crossing different Vitis species, typically vinifera with American species for disease resistance. Common hybrids include Seyval Blanc, Chambourcin, and Marquette. They're important in cold climates and humid regions where vinifera struggles. EU wine laws largely restrict hybrid use in quality wine.

terms.short_definition: Natural yeasts present on grapes or in the winery.
terms.full_definition: Indigenous (native, wild, or ambient) yeasts are naturally present on grape skins and in wineries, as opposed to commercial cultured yeasts. Fermentation with indigenous yeast is slower and less predictable but can produce more complex, terroir-expressive wines. Risk includes stuck fermentation and off-flavors.

terms.short_definition: The strength or concentration of aromas and flavors.
terms.full_definition: Intensity measures how powerful or concentrated aromas and flavors are, separate from their quality or complexity. A wine can be intensely aromatic but simple, or subtle but complex. Intensity is evaluated on the nose and palate separately. Low intensity can indicate a neutral wine or one that's closed.

terms.short_definition: Artificial watering of vineyards.
terms.full_definition: Irrigation provides water to vineyards where rainfall is insufficient. Methods include drip (most precise), furrow, and sprinkler systems. Deficit irrigation deliberately restricts water to stress vines and concentrate flavors. Irrigation is prohibited in many European appellations but essential in California, Australia, and other warm regions.

terms.short_definition: Cooked, concentrated fruit flavors like jam.
terms.full_definition: Jammy describes wines with very ripe, concentrated fruit character reminiscent of cooked jam rather than fresh fruit. It often indicates hot-climate or late-harvest fruit. Can be positive (concentrated, rich) or negative (overripe, lacking freshness). Associated with high-alcohol, low-acid styles.

terms.short_definition: Young Spanish wine with no oak aging.
terms.full_definition: Joven ('young') is a Spanish wine classification for wines released without required oak aging, meant for immediate consumption. These wines showcase fresh fruit character without oak influence. In Rioja, Joven wines must be sold within a year of harvest. Compare with Crianza, Reserva, and Gran Reserva.

terms.short_definition: The softer acid produced during malolactic fermentation.
terms.full_definition: Lactic acid is produced when malic acid is converted during malolactic fermentation. It's softer and creamier than malic acid, contributing to buttery, rounded character in wines that undergo ML. The same acid found in dairy products. Excessive lactic character can seem oily or yogurt-like.

terms.short_definition: Wine from grapes picked after normal harvest for extra ripeness.
terms.full_definition: Late harvest wines are made from grapes left on the vine past normal harvest to concentrate sugars, often becoming sweet wines. The grapes may dehydrate on the vine or develop botrytis (noble rot). German terms include Spatlese (late harvest) and Auslese (select harvest). Not all late harvest wines are sweet.

terms.short_definition: Dead yeast cells and grape solids that settle after fermentation.
terms.full_definition: Lees are sediment consisting of dead yeast cells, grape particles, and tartrates that settle after fermentation. Fine lees are smaller particles; gross lees are larger. Aging on lees (sur lie) adds texture, complexity, and protection from oxidation. Lees stirring (batonnage) enhances these effects.

terms.short_definition: The Cabernet-dominant side of Bordeaux.
terms.full_definition: The Left Bank refers to Bordeaux wine regions west of the Gironde estuary and Garonne River, including Medoc, Graves, Pessac-Leognan, and Sauternes. Gravelly soils favor Cabernet Sauvignon, which dominates blends. The 1855 Classification covers Left Bank estates. Wines are typically more structured than Right Bank.

terms.short_definition: The droplets that run down the glass after swirling.
terms.full_definition: Legs (tears or curtains) are the droplets of wine that cling to the glass and slowly run down after swirling. They're caused by the Marangoni effect - differences in surface tension between alcohol and water. Slow, thick legs indicate higher alcohol or sugar content. Legs don't indicate quality.

terms.short_definition: How long flavors persist after swallowing.
terms.full_definition: Length measures how long wine flavors linger on the palate after swallowing. A long finish (30+ seconds) generally indicates quality. Some use 'caudalies' - one caudalie equals one second of aftertaste. Long length suggests concentration and complexity. Short length often indicates dilute or simple wine.

terms.short_definition: A named vineyard site, particularly in France.
terms.full_definition: Lieu-dit ('named place') is a French cadastral term for a specific named location, often appearing on wine labels to indicate grape source. Unlike climat (Burgundy) or cru, lieu-dit doesn't necessarily imply quality classification - it simply identifies the site. Common in Loire Valley and Alsace labeling.

terms.short_definition: Wine with minimal weight and delicate texture.
terms.full_definition: Light-bodied wines feel delicate and ethereal on the palate, typically lower in alcohol, extract, and tannin. Examples include Muscadet, Vinho Verde, German Riesling, and Beaujolais. Light body isn't a fault - it's a style that excels as aperitifs and with delicate foods. Compare to medium and full-bodied.

terms.short_definition: Wine with vibrant acidity and energy.
terms.full_definition: Lively describes wines with energetic acidity and fresh character that make the wine feel active and refreshing. It implies good acid-fruit balance and a sense of vibrancy. Lively wines are typically young and fresh. The opposite would be tired, flat, or dull.

terms.short_definition: Soaking grape skins in juice to extract color, tannin, and flavor.
terms.full_definition: Maceration is the contact between grape juice/wine and grape skins, extracting color, tannin, and flavor compounds. For reds, it occurs during and sometimes after fermentation. Duration ranges from days to months depending on desired style. Extended maceration produces deeper color and firmer tannins. White wines typically have minimal skin contact.

terms.short_definition: Wine showing oxidation and heat damage, like Madeira.
terms.full_definition: Madeirized describes a fault where wine has been damaged by heat and/or oxidation, developing brown color and cooked, nutty, caramelized flavors - resembling Madeira wine. It typically results from poor storage at high temperatures. While intentional in Madeira production, it's a serious fault in table wines.

terms.short_definition: A 1.5-liter bottle, holding two standard bottles.
terms.full_definition: A magnum is a 1.5-liter wine bottle, equivalent to two standard 750ml bottles. Magnums are considered ideal for aging because the lower ratio of oxygen (through cork) to wine volume allows slower, more graceful development. They're also impressive for entertaining, serving 10-12 glasses.

terms.short_definition: A tart acid in grapes, converted to lactic acid during ML.
terms.full_definition: Malic acid is one of the two main grape acids (with tartaric), responsible for sharp, green apple-like acidity. It decreases as grapes ripen. Malolactic fermentation converts malic to softer lactic acid, reducing tartness. Winemakers may encourage or prevent ML depending on desired style - blocking it preserves freshness in aromatic whites.

terms.short_definition: Bacterial conversion of tart malic acid to softer lactic acid.
terms.full_definition: Malolactic fermentation (ML or MLF) is a bacterial (not yeast) conversion of sharp malic acid to softer lactic acid plus CO2. It reduces acidity, adds buttery diacetyl character, and increases stability. Standard for most red wines and oaked Chardonnay. Often blocked in aromatic whites to preserve freshness.

terms.short_definition: The most prestigious wine qualification worldwide.
terms.full_definition: Master of Wine (MW) is the highest professional wine qualification, awarded by the Institute of Masters of Wine in London. The program requires passing theory exams, a blind tasting exam, and submitting a research paper. Only about 420 MWs exist worldwide. It takes most candidates 3-5 years to complete.

terms.short_definition: The development and aging of wine.
terms.full_definition: Maturation encompasses all aging processes that develop wine's character - in barrel, tank, or bottle. During maturation, tannins polymerize, colors evolve, and tertiary aromas develop. The optimal maturation period varies widely by wine style. Some wines are released young; others require decades to reach peak maturity.

terms.short_definition: Wine with moderate weight between light and full.
terms.full_definition: Medium-bodied wines have moderate weight and texture on the palate, between light and full-bodied extremes. Examples include red Burgundy, Chianti, Cotes du Rhone, and Merlot. Medium body is often the most food-versatile style, neither overwhelming nor disappearing alongside food.

terms.short_definition: American Bordeaux-style blend from authorized grapes.
terms.full_definition: Meritage is a trademarked term for American wines made from traditional Bordeaux varieties. Red Meritage uses Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, Cabernet Franc, Petit Verdot, and/or Malbec. White Meritage uses Sauvignon Blanc, Semillon, and/or Muscadelle. No single variety can exceed 90%. The name combines 'merit' and 'heritage.'

terms.short_definition: Traditional bottle-fermented sparkling wine method.
terms.full_definition: Methode Champenoise (traditional method, methode traditionnelle) creates sparkling wine through secondary fermentation in the bottle. Base wine is bottled with yeast and sugar; fermentation creates bubbles; wines age on lees; riddling collects sediment; disgorgement removes it. Only Champagne can use the term 'methode champenoise' - others say 'traditional method.'

terms.short_definition: Controlled oxygen addition during winemaking.
terms.full_definition: Micro-oxygenation (micro-ox or MOX) introduces controlled amounts of oxygen through ceramic diffusers during tank aging. It can accelerate tannin polymerization, stabilize color, and reduce reduction - mimicking some barrel aging effects without oak flavor. Developed in Madiran to tame Tannat's aggressive tannins. Controversial among purists.

terms.short_definition: The specific climate conditions of a small vineyard area.
terms.full_definition: Microclimate refers to climate conditions specific to a small area - even a single row or vine - differing from the broader regional climate. Factors include elevation, slope, aspect, proximity to water, and surrounding vegetation. Understanding microclimates allows optimal variety placement and vineyard management decisions.

terms.short_definition: Aromas or flavors suggesting stones or minerals.
terms.full_definition: Minerality describes wine characteristics evoking wet stones, chalk, flint, slate, or other mineral-like qualities. It's debated whether these come from soil minerals (largely disproven), fermentation compounds, or reduction. Commonly noted in Chablis, German Riesling, and Santorini Assyrtiko. The term remains popular despite scientific skepticism.

terms.short_definition: A vineyard owned entirely by one producer.
terms.full_definition: Monopole refers to a vineyard under single ownership, producing wines from only one estate. In Burgundy's fragmented landscape, monopoles are rare and valuable. Famous examples include Romanee-Conti (DRC), La Tache, Clos de Tart, and La Romanee. Monopole status allows consistent expression of terroir without blending decisions.

terms.short_definition: The foam and bubble texture of sparkling wine.
terms.full_definition: Mousse describes the bubbles and foam in sparkling wine - their size, persistence, and texture. Fine mousse has tiny, persistent bubbles creating creamy texture. Coarse mousse has larger, more aggressive bubbles. Quality sparkling wines, especially those aged on lees, typically show finer mousse. The mousse also releases aromas.

terms.short_definition: The tactile sensations of wine in the mouth.
terms.full_definition: Mouthfeel encompasses all tactile (non-flavor) sensations in the mouth: weight, texture, temperature, tannin grip, alcohol warmth, acidity prickling, and effervescence. It's distinct from taste and aroma. Descriptors include silky, velvety, chalky, grippy, creamy, chewy, or oily. Mouthfeel is important for food pairing.

terms.short_definition: Fresh grape juice before or during fermentation.
terms.full_definition: Must is the freshly pressed grape juice containing skins, seeds, and sometimes stems before or during fermentation. It includes everything that will become wine. Must weight measures sugar content. Once fermentation completes, must becomes wine. The term comes from Latin 'mustum' (new or fresh).

terms.short_definition: Wine made with minimal intervention and additives.
terms.full_definition: Natural wine is made with minimal intervention - typically organic or biodynamic grapes, indigenous yeast fermentation, no or minimal sulfur additions, and no other additives or technological manipulation. There's no legal definition, leading to debate. Proponents value authenticity; critics note inconsistency and faults. The natural wine movement has grown significantly since 2000.

terms.short_definition: A merchant who buys grapes or wine to blend and sell.
terms.full_definition: A negociant buys grapes, juice, or finished wine from growers, then blends, ages, bottles, and sells under their own label. Important in Burgundy, Champagne, and Bordeaux. Large houses like Louis Jadot or Louis Latour are negociants. Contrast with grower-producers who make wine from their own vineyards.

terms.short_definition: Barrels being used for the first time.
terms.full_definition: New oak refers to barrels being used for the first time, imparting maximum oak flavors - vanilla, spice, toast, and cedar. After first use, barrels become 'second fill,' 'third fill,' etc., contributing progressively less oak character. '100% new oak' means all wine aged in new barrels. Over-oaking can overwhelm wine character.

terms.short_definition: Wine regions outside traditional European areas.
terms.full_definition: New World refers to wine-producing regions outside Europe's traditional Old World: primarily the Americas, Australia, New Zealand, and South Africa. The term also describes a style - riper fruit, higher alcohol, more oak, less earthy. These distinctions are increasingly blurred as winemaking becomes more global.

terms.short_definition: Beneficial fungus (Botrytis cinerea) for sweet wines.
terms.full_definition: Noble rot is the beneficial form of Botrytis cinerea that concentrates grape sugars by dehydrating berries while adding distinctive honey, apricot, and marmalade flavors. It requires specific conditions: morning humidity (fog), afternoon dryness. Essential for Sauternes, Tokaji Aszu, and German TBA/BA wines.

terms.short_definition: The overall aromatic character of wine.
terms.full_definition: Nose refers to all aromatic characteristics detected by smelling wine, encompassing both aroma (varietal character) and bouquet (fermentation and aging character). 'On the nose' describes what's smelled vs. tasted. A wine's nose can be aromatic, closed, expressive, subtle, or complex. Professional tasters evaluate nose before palate.

terms.short_definition: Wood used for barrels, adding flavor and texture to wine.
terms.full_definition: Oak barrels are used to ferment and age wine, contributing flavors (vanilla, spice, toast, caramel), tannins, and allowing slow oxygen exchange. French and American oak are most common; Hungarian, Slavonian, and others also used. Oak impact depends on barrel size, age, and toast level. Oak alternatives include chips, staves, and powder.

terms.short_definition: Pronounced oak character in wine.
terms.full_definition: Oaky describes wines with prominent oak-derived flavors - vanilla, toast, caramel, coconut, or char - that may dominate fruit character. Can be intentional style (some New World wines) or considered excessive. Modern trends favor more restrained oak. Over-oaking is a common criticism of wines from the 1990s-2000s.

terms.short_definition: The science of winemaking.
terms.full_definition: Oenology (or enology) is the scientific study of wine and winemaking, encompassing chemistry, microbiology, fermentation science, and cellar practices. An oenologist or winemaker applies these sciences professionally. Major oenology programs exist at UC Davis, University of Bordeaux, Geisenheim, and Adelaide.

terms.short_definition: Wine with slight perceptible sweetness.
terms.full_definition: Off-dry wines have slight residual sugar - enough to perceive as faintly sweet but not overtly sweet. Typically 5-20 g/L residual sugar depending on acidity. Common in Riesling, Chenin Blanc, and many Champagnes. High acidity can mask sweetness, making wines taste drier than sugar levels suggest. Also called 'semi-dry.'

terms.short_definition: Mature grapevines, typically 35+ years old.
terms.full_definition: Old vines (vieilles vignes) are mature grapevines, though no legal definition exists for minimum age. Generally 35-50+ years, sometimes over 100. Old vines naturally reduce yields, develop deep root systems, and produce concentrated, complex wines. They're valued for intensity but are less productive and harder to maintain.

terms.short_definition: Traditional European wine regions.
terms.full_definition: Old World refers to traditional European wine regions with centuries of winemaking history: France, Italy, Spain, Portugal, Germany, Austria, and others. The term also describes a style - higher acidity, lower alcohol, more earthy/mineral, less fruit-forward than New World. Old World regions emphasize terroir and tradition over technology.

terms.short_definition: Relating to the sense of smell.
terms.full_definition: Olfactory refers to smell perception, crucial in wine evaluation since most 'flavor' is actually aroma perceived through orthonasal (sniffing) and retronasal (from palate) pathways. Humans can distinguish thousands of odors. Olfactory fatigue can occur during extended tasting, reducing sensitivity.

terms.short_definition: White wine made with extended skin contact.
terms.full_definition: Orange wine is white wine fermented with extended skin contact (days to months), extracting color, tannin, and phenolics like red wine production. The resulting amber/orange color gives the name. Ancient in Georgia and Slovenia, it's been revived by natural wine producers. Styles range from subtle amber to deeply tannic.

terms.short_definition: Farming without synthetic chemicals.
terms.full_definition: Organic viticulture avoids synthetic pesticides, herbicides, and fertilizers, relying on natural alternatives and ecological management. Organic wine certification varies by country - EU allows limited sulfites, US 'organic' allows none (vs. 'made with organic grapes'). Many quality estates farm organically without certification.

terms.short_definition: Chemical reaction with oxygen, aging or spoiling wine.
terms.full_definition: Oxidation is the chemical reaction between wine and oxygen. Controlled oxidation is essential for aging, softening tannins and developing complexity. Excessive oxidation causes spoilage: browning color, flat aromas, sherry-like character, and loss of fruit. Intentional oxidative aging occurs in Sherry, Madeira, and some Jura wines.

terms.short_definition: The taste and texture sensations of wine in the mouth.
terms.full_definition: Palate refers to taste and mouthfeel sensations when wine is in the mouth, as opposed to nose (aroma). It includes flavors (sweet, sour, bitter, salty, umami), texture, temperature, tannin, and alcohol warmth. Professional tasting evaluates attack (first impression), mid-palate, and finish separately.

terms.short_definition: A specific plot of vineyard land.
terms.full_definition: Parcelle (or parcel) refers to a specific defined plot within a vineyard, often with distinct characteristics. Single-parcel wines showcase one plot's terroir. In Burgundy's fragmented ownership, one domaine may own parcels in dozens of different vineyards. Modern precision viticulture manages parcels individually.

terms.short_definition: The quality and behavior of bubbles in sparkling wine.
terms.full_definition: Perlage describes the bubbles in sparkling wine - their size, quantity, persistence, and movement. Fine perlage features tiny, numerous, persistent bubbles rising in steady streams (trains). Coarse perlage has larger, fewer, quicker-dissipating bubbles. Quality correlates with production method and aging time on lees.

terms.short_definition: Kerosene-like aroma in aged Riesling.
terms.full_definition: Petrol (kerosene, diesel) is an aroma that develops in aged Riesling, caused by 1,1,6-trimethyl-1,2-dihydronaphthalene (TDN). It ranges from subtle honey-petroleum to pronounced diesel. Loved by some, disliked by others. It's more pronounced in warm-climate Rieslings and increases with age. Considered varietal character, not a fault.

terms.short_definition: Measure of acidity strength in wine.
terms.full_definition: pH measures hydrogen ion concentration, indicating acidity strength (not amount). Wine pH typically ranges from 3.0 (high acid) to 4.0 (low acid). Lower pH increases color stability, microbial resistance, and aging potential. pH differs from total acidity (TA) which measures acid quantity. Both metrics matter for wine balance.

terms.short_definition: Chemical compounds affecting color, texture, and flavor.
terms.full_definition: Phenolics are a large class of compounds including tannins, anthocyanins (color), and flavonoids. They contribute to wine color, structure, mouthfeel, and aging ability. Extracted from skins, seeds, and stems during maceration, and from oak during barrel aging. Total phenolics are measured and influence wine style and longevity.

terms.short_definition: Root-feeding pest that devastated European vineyards.
terms.full_definition: Phylloxera (Daktulosphaira vitifoliae) is a root-feeding aphid native to North America that devastated European vineyards in the late 1800s. European Vitis vinifera has no resistance; American species do. The solution was grafting vinifera onto American rootstocks. Phylloxera reshaped global viticulture and remains present worldwide.

terms.short_definition: Punching down the cap during fermentation.
terms.full_definition: Pigeage (punch-down) is a cap management technique where the floating layer of grape skins is pushed down into the fermenting juice, either manually with a tool or mechanically. This extracts color, tannin, and flavor while preventing the cap from drying and spoiling. Gentler than pump-over (remontage).

terms.short_definition: Fortified sweet wine from Portugal's Douro Valley.
terms.full_definition: Port is a fortified wine from Portugal's Douro Valley, made by adding grape spirit during fermentation to preserve sweetness. Styles include Ruby (young, fruity), Tawny (oxidatively aged), Vintage (single vintage), LBV (Late Bottled Vintage), and Vintage Port (the finest, long-aging style). Traditional grapes include Touriga Nacional, Touriga Franca, and Tinta Roriz.

terms.short_definition: The second-highest vineyard classification in Burgundy.
terms.full_definition: Premier Cru ('first growth') is the second tier of Burgundy vineyard classification, below Grand Cru. These vineyards appear on labels with the village name plus vineyard (e.g., 'Gevrey-Chambertin Les Cazetiers'). There are over 600 Premier Cru vineyards. In Bordeaux, Premier Cru confusingly means the top level (First Growth).

terms.short_definition: Wine extracted by pressing grape skins after fermentation.
terms.full_definition: Press wine is extracted by mechanically pressing grape skins after fermentation drains the free-run wine. It's typically darker, more tannic, and more extracted than free-run. Press fractions are often kept separate and blended back selectively to add structure. Heavy pressing extracts harsh tannins and bitterness.

terms.short_definition: Extracting juice or wine from grapes using pressure.
terms.full_definition: Pressing extracts juice from grapes (for whites) or wine from fermented skins (for reds). Press types include basket, bladder (pneumatic), and continuous screw presses. Gentle pressing extracts cleaner juice; harder pressing adds phenolics. Multiple press fractions may be kept separate and blended selectively.

terms.short_definition: Aromas derived from the grape variety itself.
terms.full_definition: Primary aromas come from the grape variety - the characteristic scents that define varietal identity. Examples: Riesling's citrus and petrol, Sauvignon Blanc's grass and grapefruit, Cabernet's cassis. Primary aromas are strongest in young wines and are distinct from secondary (fermentation) and tertiary (aging) aromas.

terms.short_definition: Cutting back vine wood to control growth and yield.
terms.full_definition: Pruning removes vine wood during dormancy to shape the plant and control yield. Winter pruning determines how many buds (future shoots and clusters) remain. Cane pruning (Guyot) retains entire canes; spur pruning (cordon) keeps short spurs. Pruning decisions affect vine balance, fruit quality, and vine longevity.

terms.short_definition: A large oak barrel, typically 450-500 liters.
terms.full_definition: A puncheon is a large barrel holding approximately 450-500 liters (about twice a barrique). The larger size reduces oak influence per volume and slows oxidation. Common in the Rhone Valley, Australia, and for wines where subtle oak is preferred. Also spelled 'ponchon.'

terms.short_definition: Transferring wine off its sediment.
terms.full_definition: Racking transfers wine from one container to another, leaving behind settled sediment (lees). This clarifies wine and prevents off-odors from decomposing lees. Multiple rackings may occur during elevage. Less racking maintains more texture and freshness (sur lie style). Air contact during racking can be minimized or maximized depending on goals.

terms.short_definition: Wine with vibrant, energetic acidity.
terms.full_definition: Racy describes wines with pronounced, energetic acidity that creates a sense of tension and vitality. It implies a lively, exciting quality - the wine has racing energy. Typically applied to high-acid whites like Riesling or Champagne. Racy wines feel tense and alive, demanding attention.

terms.short_definition: Sulfur compounds from lack of oxygen, creating off-odors.
terms.full_definition: Reduction is the opposite of oxidation - chemical reactions in oxygen-absent conditions producing sulfur compounds. Mild reduction smells of struck match or rubber; severe reduction of rotten eggs or sewage. It can occur from screwcap closure, minimal oxygen winemaking, or sulfur residues. Often blows off with aeration.

terms.short_definition: Gradually rotating sparkling wine bottles to collect sediment.
terms.full_definition: Remuage (riddling) is the gradual rotation and tilting of sparkling wine bottles to move yeast sediment into the neck for removal (disgorgement). Traditional hand-riddling on pupitres takes 6-8 weeks; automated gyropalettes accomplish it in days. Essential to traditional method sparkling wine production.

terms.short_definition: A designation suggesting higher quality or longer aging.
terms.full_definition: Reserve implies special selection, longer aging, or higher quality, but meaning varies by country. In Spain and Italy (Reserva/Riserva), it mandates minimum aging. In the US, it has no legal definition - it's marketing. Reserve wines may or may not be superior to non-reserve. Always check regional regulations.

terms.short_definition: Sugar remaining in wine after fermentation.
