terms.full_definition: Residual sugar (RS) is unfermented grape sugar remaining in finished wine, measured in grams per liter. Dry wines have <4 g/L; off-dry 4-12 g/L; medium-sweet 12-45 g/L; sweet >45 g/L. RS can balance high acidity - wines with high acid and high RS may taste drier than numbers suggest.

terms.short_definition: Antioxidant compound found in grape skins.
terms.full_definition: Resveratrol is a polyphenol compound in grape skins, concentrated in red wines due to skin contact. It gained fame from studies suggesting health benefits including heart protection and longevity. While wine contains resveratrol, the amounts are small compared to supplements. The 'French Paradox' popularized wine's health reputation.

terms.short_definition: Perceiving aromas through the back of the throat.
terms.full_definition: Retronasal refers to perceiving aromas through the passage connecting throat to nasal cavity, as wine is swallowed or held in mouth. Most of what we call 'taste' is actually retronasal smell. It's why wine tastes different from how it smells, and why holding your nose reduces flavor perception.

terms.short_definition: Rotating bottles to move sediment for disgorgement.
terms.full_definition: Riddling (remuage) gradually rotates and tilts sparkling wine bottles to collect yeast sediment in the neck. Traditional hand-riddling uses A-frame wooden racks called pupitres. Modern gyropalettes automate the process. The sediment-laden neck is then frozen for clean removal during disgorgement.

terms.short_definition: Noble white grape known for acidity and aging potential.
terms.full_definition: Riesling is considered one of the world's greatest white grapes, producing wines from bone-dry to intensely sweet. Germany's signature variety, it's also important in Alsace, Austria, Australia, and parts of the US. Hallmarks include high acidity, aromatic intensity, and exceptional aging ability with development of petrol notes.

terms.short_definition: The Merlot-dominant side of Bordeaux.
terms.full_definition: The Right Bank refers to Bordeaux wine regions east of the Gironde/Dordogne rivers: Saint-Emilion, Pomerol, and satellites. Clay and limestone soils favor Merlot, which dominates blends. Wines are typically softer and earlier-maturing than Left Bank Cabernet-based wines. Famous estates include Petrus, Le Pin, and Cheval Blanc.

terms.short_definition: Fully mature fruit character in grapes or wine.
terms.full_definition: Ripe describes optimal grape maturity - sugars developed, acids balanced, tannins polymerized, flavors full. In tasting, it indicates generous, developed fruit without green or underripe notes. Overripe grapes lose acidity and freshness. 'Physiological ripeness' considers tannin and flavor, not just sugar.

terms.short_definition: Root system onto which fruiting vines are grafted.
terms.full_definition: Rootstock is the root system onto which fruiting grape varieties are grafted. After phylloxera devastated European vines, American species (resistant to the pest) became universal rootstocks. Different rootstocks affect vigor, drought tolerance, soil adaptation, and even wine character. Most vines worldwide are grafted.

terms.short_definition: Pink wine from limited skin contact with red grapes.
terms.full_definition: Rose (pink wine) is made from red grapes with limited skin contact - either direct pressing, short maceration, or saignee (bleeding). Color ranges from pale salmon to deep coral depending on technique and grape variety. Dry Provence rose dominates the market, but styles range from off-dry to sweet, still to sparkling.

terms.short_definition: Smooth, full, well-balanced without hard edges.
terms.full_definition: Round describes wines with smooth, complete texture lacking sharp angles or aggressive tannins. It suggests soft curves rather than hard edges - the wine feels harmonious and complete in the mouth. Round wines may be ready to drink earlier than angular, tannic wines.

terms.short_definition: Bleeding off juice from red wine tanks to make rose.
terms.full_definition: Saignee ('bleeding') drains a portion of juice from red wine fermentation tanks early, before significant extraction. This juice becomes rose, while the remaining red wine gains concentration from higher skin-to-juice ratio. Saignee rose tends to be darker and more structured than direct-press rose.

terms.short_definition: Italy's most planted red grape, the base of Chianti.
terms.full_definition: Sangiovese is Italy's most important red grape, the backbone of Chianti, Brunello di Montalcino, Vino Nobile, and many Super Tuscans. It produces wines with high acidity, firm tannins, and cherry-tomato-herb character. Highly site-sensitive, it expresses terroir distinctively. Sangiovese Grosso (large Sangiovese) is the Brunello clone.

terms.short_definition: Non-fruit flavors like herbs, earth, and umami.
terms.full_definition: Savory describes non-sweet, non-fruit flavors - herbs, earth, mushroom, meat, olive, soy, or umami character. Savory wines emphasize these elements over fruit. Associated with Old World styles and aged wines. Savory character often develops with bottle age as fruit fades.

terms.short_definition: Metal closure alternative to cork.
terms.full_definition: Screwcap (Stelvin is the leading brand) is an aluminum closure with a liner controlling oxygen transmission. Advantages include no cork taint, consistent aging, and easy opening. Disadvantages include potential reduction in some wines and perception issues. Now standard in Australia and New Zealand, gaining acceptance elsewhere.

terms.short_definition: Aromas from fermentation and winemaking.
terms.full_definition: Secondary aromas develop during fermentation and winemaking - yeast character (bread, brioche), malolactic conversion (butter, cream), and oak aging (vanilla, toast, spice). They're distinct from primary (grape) and tertiary (bottle age) aromas. Most evident in young wines that have completed barrel aging.

terms.short_definition: Solid particles that settle in wine over time.
terms.full_definition: Sediment consists of solid particles that precipitate in wine, including tartrate crystals, color pigments, tannin complexes, and dead yeast. It's natural in aged wines and unfiltered wines. Sediment is harmless but can be gritty or bitter. Decanting or careful pouring separates wine from sediment.

terms.short_definition: Partial whole-grape fermentation technique.
terms.full_definition: Semi-carbonic maceration (whole-cluster fermentation) places whole grape clusters in tanks where weight crushes bottom grapes, beginning conventional fermentation. Upper grapes undergo intracellular carbonic maceration. This hybrid technique is used for some Beaujolais, Rioja, and natural wines, producing fruity character with more complexity than full carbonic.

terms.short_definition: Fortified wine from Jerez, Spain.
terms.full_definition: Sherry is a fortified wine from the Jerez region of southern Spain, made primarily from Palomino grapes. Styles include biological aging (Fino, Manzanilla - aged under flor yeast) and oxidative aging (Oloroso, Amontillado, Palo Cortado). Sweet sherries are made by blending with Pedro Ximenez or sweetening wines. All sherries use the solera system.

terms.short_definition: Smooth, refined texture like silk fabric.
terms.full_definition: Silky describes exceptionally smooth, fine-textured wines - tannins so refined they feel like silk on the palate. It's a highly positive descriptor indicating polish and elegance. Silky texture is prized in fine Burgundy, premium Merlot, and well-aged wines. The opposite of coarse or grainy.

terms.short_definition: Wine made from grapes of one specific vineyard.
terms.full_definition: Single vineyard wines come exclusively from one named vineyard site, showcasing that location's terroir. The term has no strict legal definition in most regions. Single vineyard wines are often a producer's premium offerings, demonstrating site-specific character. Burgundy's climat system formalizes this approach.

terms.short_definition: Leaving juice in contact with grape skins.
terms.full_definition: Skin contact describes leaving crushed grapes' juice touching the skins. For red wines, extended skin contact extracts color and tannin during maceration. For whites, brief skin contact (a few hours) can add texture and complexity. Extended skin contact on white grapes produces orange wine.

terms.short_definition: Wine without rough edges or aggressive tannins.
terms.full_definition: Smooth describes wines without sharp angles, harsh tannins, or biting acidity. The texture feels polished and easy. Smoothness can indicate well-integrated components, ripe fruit, or appropriate aging. Overly smooth wines may be criticized as simple or lacking structure.

terms.short_definition: Low in tannin or acidity, easy drinking.
terms.full_definition: Soft describes wines low in tannin and/or acidity, creating a gentle, approachable mouthfeel. It can be positive (easy-drinking, smooth) or negative (flabby, lacking structure). Soft wines are typically ready to drink young and pair with lighter foods. The opposite of firm, angular, or structured.

terms.short_definition: Fractional blending system for consistent aged character.
terms.full_definition: Solera is a fractional blending system where wine is partially transferred through a series of barrels containing progressively older wine. New wine enters the youngest level; final product is drawn from the oldest. This maintains consistent style while incorporating aged character. Essential for Sherry and some Madeira production.

terms.short_definition: Wine professional specializing in service and selection.
terms.full_definition: A sommelier is a trained wine professional responsible for wine programs at restaurants, managing purchasing, storage, list curation, and service. Certification programs (Court of Master Sommeliers, ASI) provide credentials. Top sommeliers have encyclopedic knowledge and refined blind tasting skills. The role has expanded to other beverages.

terms.short_definition: German late-harvest wine designation.
terms.full_definition: Spatlese ('late harvest') is a German Pradikat level indicating grapes picked at least a week after normal harvest, with higher must weights. Wines can be dry (trocken) or off-dry/sweet. Spatlese represents the beginning of late-harvest styles, above Kabinett and below Auslese. It's often a sweet spot for quality and value.

terms.short_definition: Aromas or flavors of culinary spices.
terms.full_definition: Spicy describes aromas and flavors of spices - pepper (black, white, pink), clove, cinnamon, anise, nutmeg, ginger. Some varieties are naturally spicy (Syrah's black pepper, Gewurztraminer's spice). Oak aging can add spice notes. Spiciness differs from heat from alcohol.

terms.short_definition: Non-sparkling wine without carbonation.
terms.full_definition: Still wine is non-sparkling wine - without intentional carbonation or residual CO2. The vast majority of wine is still. Some still wines have slight spritz (prickle) from dissolved CO2, either residual from fermentation or intentionally added for freshness (common in Vinho Verde).

terms.short_definition: The framework of acid, tannin, and alcohol in wine.
terms.full_definition: Structure refers to wine's backbone - the framework of acidity, tannin, and alcohol that supports fruit and other flavors. Good structure suggests aging potential and food-friendliness. Structural components should be balanced. Wines can be described as well-structured (balanced framework) or lacking structure (too soft).

terms.short_definition: Fermentation that stops before all sugar is consumed.
terms.full_definition: Stuck fermentation occurs when yeast dies or becomes inactive before converting all sugar to alcohol, leaving unintended residual sugar. Causes include temperature extremes, nutrient deficiency, high alcohol toxicity, or wild yeast failure. Restarting requires adding fresh yeast and carefully managing conditions.

terms.short_definition: Sulfur compounds used to preserve wine.
terms.full_definition: Sulfites (sulfur dioxide, SO2) are added to wine as antioxidant and antimicrobial preservatives. All wine contains some natural sulfites from fermentation. Added sulfites prevent oxidation and spoilage. 'Contains Sulfites' labeling is required above 10 ppm. Sweet wines need more SO2; reds need less than whites. Some people are sensitive.

terms.short_definition: Premium Tuscan wines using non-traditional varieties.
terms.full_definition: Super Tuscan refers to premium Tuscan wines that broke DOC rules, typically using Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, or 100% Sangiovese, often in barriques. Pioneers like Sassicaia and Tignanello in the 1970s proved Italian wines could match Bordeaux quality. Many were labeled IGT Toscana. Some are now DOC Bolgheri.

terms.short_definition: Aging wine on its lees for texture and complexity.
terms.full_definition: Sur lie ('on the lees') aging keeps wine in contact with dead yeast cells after fermentation. This adds body, creaminess, and complexity through autolysis - yeast cell breakdown releasing compounds. Important for Muscadet (where it's legally defined), white Burgundy, and Champagne. Often combined with batonnage.

terms.short_definition: Environmentally conscious farming practices.
terms.full_definition: Sustainable viticulture balances environmental, social, and economic considerations. It may include reduced chemical use, water conservation, biodiversity, carbon footprint reduction, and worker welfare. Various certification programs exist (SIP, LIVE, Sustainable Winegrowing). Less strict than organic but allows more flexibility.

terms.short_definition: Wine with noticeable residual sugar.
terms.full_definition: Sweet wines have significant residual sugar, usually above 45 g/L. Sweetness ranges from lightly sweet to lusciously sweet. Sweet wines balance sugar with acidity to avoid cloying. Made by stopping fermentation (Port), concentrating by botrytis, freezing (Eiswein), or drying grapes. Dessert wines, late harvest, and fortified styles are typically sweet.

terms.short_definition: Red grape known for dark fruit and peppery spice.
terms.full_definition: Syrah (Shiraz in Australia) produces powerful red wines with dark fruit, black pepper, and meaty, smoky character. Native to France's Northern Rhone (Hermitage, Cote-Rotie), it thrives in warm climates worldwide. Styles range from elegant and peppery (cool climate) to rich and chocolatey (warm climate). Key component of GSM blends.

terms.short_definition: Polyphenols creating dry, astringent mouthfeel.
terms.full_definition: Tannins are polyphenolic compounds from grape skins, seeds, stems, and oak barrels. They create astringency - the drying, gripping sensation in your mouth - and contribute to structure, aging potential, and color stability. Tannins polymerize and soften with age. Ripe tannins are velvety; unripe tannins are green and bitter.

terms.short_definition: The primary acid in wine grapes.
terms.full_definition: Tartaric acid is the principal grape acid, relatively stable unlike malic acid which breaks down with heat or malolactic fermentation. It provides crisp, clean acidity essential for balance and preservation. In cold conditions, it precipitates as harmless tartrate crystals (wine diamonds) on corks or bottle bottoms.

terms.short_definition: Harmless crystals that precipitate from wine.
terms.full_definition: Tartrates (wine diamonds) are crystalline deposits of potassium bitartrate that form when wine is chilled. They're completely harmless and indicate the wine wasn't heavily cold-stabilized. Often seen on corks or as sediment. More common in premium wines that minimize intervention.

terms.short_definition: Chemical compound causing cork taint.
terms.full_definition: TCA (2,4,6-trichloroanisole) is the primary compound responsible for cork taint, creating musty, wet cardboard, or moldy aromas. It forms when natural fungi in cork interact with chlorophenol compounds. Detection threshold is extremely low (2-5 parts per trillion). TCA can also contaminate wineries, affecting corked and screwcapped wines alike.

terms.short_definition: The complete natural environment affecting wine character.
terms.full_definition: Terroir is the French concept that wine reflects its place - the complete environment including soil, climate, elevation, aspect, and microclimate. Some expand it to include human factors. Wines expressing terroir taste 'somewhere' rather than generic. Terroir is central to European wine philosophy and the appellation system.

terms.short_definition: Complex aromas developed through aging.
terms.full_definition: Tertiary aromas develop during bottle aging through slow oxidation and other reactions. They include leather, tobacco, earth, mushroom, dried fruit, and forest floor. These complex notes distinguish mature wines from young ones. As primary fruit fades, tertiary character emerges. Also called 'bottle bouquet.'

terms.short_definition: The tactile sensation of wine in the mouth.
terms.full_definition: Texture describes how wine feels physically in the mouth - silky, velvety, grainy, oily, creamy, chalky, or rough. It's determined by tannins, glycerol, acid, and extract. Texture differs from flavor; it's about touch, not taste. Fine texture suggests quality; coarse texture can be a fault.

terms.short_definition: The charring level applied to barrel interiors.
terms.full_definition: Toast refers to heat treatment applied to barrel interiors during cooperage. Light toast preserves wood character; medium toast adds vanilla, caramel; heavy/high toast contributes smoke, coffee, chocolate. Toast level affects the flavors oak imparts to wine. Coopers control toast duration and intensity.

terms.short_definition: Aromas of toast, often from oak or lees aging.
terms.full_definition: Toasty describes aromas reminiscent of browned bread or toast. Sources include oak barrel toasting, lees aging (autolysis), and extended bottle age in Champagne. Toasty character can also evoke hazelnut, brioche, or caramel. It's commonly found in oaked Chardonnay and aged sparkling wines.

terms.short_definition: The total concentration of acids in wine.
terms.full_definition: Total acidity (TA) measures the combined concentration of all acids in wine, expressed in grams per liter. It differs from pH, which measures acid strength. Wine typically ranges from 5-8 g/L TA. High TA wines taste tart; low TA wines may taste flat. Both TA and pH matter for balance assessment.

terms.short_definition: Bottle-fermented sparkling wine production.
terms.full_definition: Traditional method (methode traditionnelle, Classic method) produces sparkling wine through secondary fermentation in the bottle. After base wine bottling with yeast and sugar, bottles age on lees, are riddled, disgorged, and dosed. This is the Champagne method but can't legally use that term. Higher quality than tank method.

terms.short_definition: Supporting vine growth on wire and post systems.
terms.full_definition: Trellising trains vines onto wire systems supported by posts. Different trellis designs (VSP, GDC, Scott Henry, Lyre) position canopy differently, affecting sunlight exposure, air circulation, and fruit quality. Trellising decisions consider climate, variety, and desired wine style. Untrellis bush vines are an alternative.

terms.short_definition: How well a wine represents its type or origin.
terms.full_definition: Typicity describes how faithfully a wine represents its variety, region, or style. A typical Chablis shows Chablis character; an atypical one doesn't. Typicity is valued in traditional regions and wine competitions. Critics debate whether typicity should override quality - can an atypical wine be great?

terms.short_definition: The air space between wine and cork in a bottle.
terms.full_definition: Ullage is the fill level or air space in a bottle - the gap between wine surface and cork. Excessive ullage indicates evaporation or leakage, often from dried corks or poor storage. Fill levels decline naturally with age. High ullage (below shoulder) in older bottles suggests greater oxidation risk and reduced value.

terms.short_definition: The savory fifth taste sensation.
terms.full_definition: Umami is the fifth basic taste (with sweet, sour, salty, bitter) - a savory, brothy, meaty quality. In wine, umami comes from amino acids released during aging, particularly sur lie aging. It contributes depth and savoriness. Foods high in umami (mushrooms, aged cheese, soy) pair well with wines showing similar qualities.

terms.short_definition: Wine bottled without filtration.
terms.full_definition: Unfiltered wines skip filtration before bottling, retaining more texture, complexity, and potentially some cloudiness or sediment. Proponents argue filtration strips character; critics note potential instability. 'Unfined and unfiltered' indicates minimal intervention. Common in premium wines and natural wine production.

terms.short_definition: Wine named for its grape variety.
terms.full_definition: Varietal wine is labeled with the grape variety name (Chardonnay, Cabernet Sauvignon) rather than place. Regulations vary: US requires 75% minimum of named variety, EU requires 85%. Varietal labeling dominates New World wines, while Old World traditionally emphasizes regional names. A varietal also means a grape variety.

terms.short_definition: Aromas of vegetables or unripe grapes.
terms.full_definition: Vegetal describes aromas of vegetables - bell pepper, asparagus, green beans, or herbaceous qualities. Light vegetal character is varietal for Cabernet Sauvignon and Sauvignon Blanc (from pyrazines). Pronounced vegetal notes often indicate underripe grapes or excess vigor. Context determines if it's characteristic or fault.

terms.short_definition: Smooth, soft, luxurious texture like velvet.
terms.full_definition: Velvety describes exceptionally smooth, plush texture - tannins so fine and ripe they feel like velvet fabric. It's a highly positive descriptor indicating refined, luxurious mouthfeel. Velvety texture is prized in Merlot, aged Pinot Noir, and premium reds. Related to but more luxurious than silky.

terms.short_definition: The onset of ripening when grapes change color.
terms.full_definition: Veraison marks the beginning of grape ripening, when berries soften, accumulate sugar, lose acidity, and change color - red varieties turn from green to purple, whites become golden. It occurs 40-50 days before harvest. Uneven veraison indicates vine stress or disease. After veraison, water stress increases concentration.

terms.short_definition: Comparing multiple vintages from one producer.
terms.full_definition: A vertical tasting samples multiple vintages from a single producer, showing how wines evolve and how vintage conditions affect the wine. For example, tasting Chateau Margaux from 2010-2020. Verticals reveal aging patterns, vintage character, and winemaking evolution. The contrast is horizontal tasting (one vintage, multiple producers).

terms.short_definition: The growth energy and vegetative power of a vine.
terms.full_definition: Vigor describes a vine's vegetative growth energy - shoot length, leaf size, and overall growth rate. High-vigor vines produce excessive foliage potentially shading fruit. Low vigor concentrates energy into fewer grapes. Vigor is influenced by rootstock, soil fertility, water availability, and variety. Balanced vigor is the goal.

terms.short_definition: Wine from a named commune or village.
terms.full_definition: Village wine in Burgundy bears a village name (Gevrey-Chambertin, Meursault) without a specific vineyard. It's above regional wine but below Premier Cru and Grand Cru. Village wines can blend fruit from multiple vineyards within the commune. Some villages have better reputations than others.

terms.short_definition: Basic French wine category without appellation restrictions.
terms.full_definition: Vin de France (formerly Vin de Table) is France's basic wine classification, allowing grapes from anywhere in France without appellation restrictions. It permits varietal labeling and vintage dating. Some prestigious producers use Vin de France to escape AOC rules, producing premium wines under this humble classification.

terms.short_definition: The process of making wine from grapes.
terms.full_definition: Vinification encompasses all winemaking processes from grape reception through fermentation to the point wine is ready for aging or bottling. It includes crushing, pressing, fermentation, and immediate post-fermentation treatments. Different vinification techniques produce dramatically different wines from the same grapes.

terms.short_definition: The year grapes were harvested.
terms.full_definition: Vintage refers to the year in which grapes were harvested. Vintage dating requires minimum percentages (typically 85-100%) from that year. Non-vintage (NV) wines blend multiple years for consistency (common in Champagne and fortified wines). Vintage variation significantly impacts wine character in marginal climates.

terms.short_definition: The thickness or flowing quality of wine.
terms.full_definition: Viscosity describes how thick or thin wine flows in the glass and feels in the mouth. Higher alcohol, glycerol, and residual sugar increase viscosity. Viscous wines cling to the glass (legs/tears) and feel rich in mouth. Low viscosity suggests light, lean wine. Viscosity influences texture perception.

terms.short_definition: The science and practice of growing grapes.
terms.full_definition: Viticulture is the cultivation of grapevines for winemaking, encompassing site selection, variety choice, training systems, canopy management, disease control, irrigation, and harvest decisions. Good viticulture is considered more important than cellar work - 'wine is made in the vineyard.' A viticulturist specializes in vine growing.

terms.short_definition: The European grape species used for fine wine.
terms.full_definition: Vitis vinifera is the European grape species encompassing essentially all fine wine grapes - Cabernet Sauvignon, Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, etc. Native to Europe and Western Asia, it was nearly destroyed by phylloxera in the 1800s and now grows grafted onto American rootstocks worldwide. Other Vitis species are used for rootstocks and hybrids.

terms.short_definition: Acetic acid and related compounds that can spoil wine.
terms.full_definition: Volatile acidity (VA) refers to steam-distillable acids, primarily acetic acid and its ester ethyl acetate. All wines contain some VA, but excessive levels (above 0.7-1.2 g/L) create vinegar-like sourness and nail polish aromas. VA indicates bacterial spoilage or oxidation. Low levels can add lift and complexity.

terms.short_definition: Vertical Shoot Positioning - common vine training system.
terms.full_definition: VSP (Vertical Shoot Positioning) is the most common modern trellising system. Shoots grow upward between catch wires, creating a vertical, two-dimensional canopy. It provides excellent sunlight exposure, air circulation, and mechanization potential. VSP works well in cool to moderate climates; hot climates may need more shade.

terms.short_definition: The heaviness or lightness of wine on the palate.
terms.full_definition: Weight describes how heavy or light wine feels in the mouth - similar to body but focusing on the sensation of heft. High-alcohol, extracted wines have more weight; light, delicate wines have less. Weight influences food pairing: heavyweight wines need rich foods, lightweight wines suit delicate dishes.

terms.short_definition: Fermenting with grape stems intact.
terms.full_definition: Whole cluster fermentation includes grape stems, not just berries. Stems add tannin (green if unripe, refined if mature), aromatic complexity, and textural elements. Used for some Burgundy, Rhone, and Oregon Pinot Noir. Percentage varies from small additions to 100%. Requires fully ripe, lignified stems to avoid green character.

terms.short_definition: Defects that diminish wine quality.
terms.full_definition: Wine faults are undesirable characteristics from production problems, contamination, or poor storage. Major faults include cork taint (TCA), oxidation, volatile acidity, Brettanomyces, heat damage, reduction, and refermentation. Some faults (brett, slight oxidation) are debated - acceptable in traditional styles, unacceptable in modern context.

terms.short_definition: The person responsible for producing wine.
terms.full_definition: The winemaker (vinificateur, oenologue, cellar master) oversees all aspects of wine production - harvest decisions, fermentation, aging, blending, and bottling. Philosophy varies from interventionist (controlling every detail) to minimalist (letting terroir express itself). Many winemakers also manage viticulture; others focus solely on cellar work.

terms.short_definition: Microorganisms that convert sugar to alcohol.
terms.full_definition: Yeasts are single-celled fungi that ferment sugar into alcohol and CO2. Saccharomyces cerevisiae is the primary wine yeast. Winemakers use commercial cultured yeasts (selected for reliability, flavor profile) or indigenous wild yeasts (naturally present, more unpredictable). Yeast choice affects fermentation character and wine style.

terms.short_definition: The amount of grapes produced per vineyard area.
terms.full_definition: Yield measures grape production per area - typically hectoliters per hectare (hl/ha) or tons per acre. Low yields (under 35 hl/ha) often produce concentrated, quality-focused wines; high yields (over 60 hl/ha) can dilute character. Yields are regulated in many appellations. Old vines naturally yield less.

terms.short_definition: Wine recently made, not yet aged.
terms.full_definition: Young wines are recently produced, showing primary fruit character without developed bottle age. They may be fresh and vibrant or rough and tannic depending on style. Some wines are made for immediate consumption; others need years to mature. 'Too young' suggests a wine needs more time to integrate.

terms.short_definition: Bright, citrusy, lively character.
terms.full_definition: Zesty describes wines with bright, citrusy, energetic character - like citrus zest. It implies vibrant acidity, lemon-lime aromas, and refreshing drinkability. Commonly applied to Sauvignon Blanc, Riesling, and other aromatic whites. Zesty wines stimulate the palate and pair well with seafood and light dishes.

terms.short_definition: California's signature red grape, related to Primitivo.
terms.full_definition: Zinfandel is a red grape considered California's own, though genetically identical to Italy's Primitivo and Croatia's Tribidrag. It produces bold, jammy reds with high alcohol and berry-bramble character, plus blush White Zinfandel. Old vine Zinfandel is prized for concentration and complexity. It ripens unevenly, requiring careful viticulture.

terms.short_definition: The historic ranking of Bordeaux chateaux for the 1855 Paris Exhibition.
terms.full_definition: The 1855 Classification ranked Bordeaux Left Bank red wines (and Sauternes) for the Paris Universal Exhibition. Chateaux were classified into five growths based on price and reputation. The classification has only changed once - Mouton Rothschild's promotion to First Growth in 1973. Despite its age, it remains influential in pricing and perception.

terms.short_definition: Alcohol By Volume - the percentage of alcohol in wine.
terms.full_definition: ABV (Alcohol By Volume) measures the percentage of ethanol in a beverage. Wine typically ranges from 5.5% (Moscato d'Asti) to 16%+ (Amarone). ABV must appear on wine labels in most countries. It affects body, sweetness perception, and aging potential. Hot climates and riper grapes tend to produce higher ABV wines.

terms.short_definition: Adding acid to must or wine to increase acidity.
terms.full_definition: Acidification adds tartaric, citric, or malic acid to grape must or wine to raise acidity and lower pH. It's permitted in warm regions where grapes lose acidity during ripening. Legal in California but prohibited in many European regions where chaptalization is allowed instead. Goal is achieving better balance.

terms.short_definition: Process requiring oxygen, opposite of anaerobic.
terms.full_definition: Aerobic describes processes requiring oxygen. Acetobacter (vinegar bacteria) is aerobic and needs oxygen to convert alcohol to acetic acid. Oxidative winemaking deliberately exposes wine to air. Contrast with anaerobic fermentation, where yeast works without oxygen. Understanding aerobic vs anaerobic is key to winemaking decisions.

terms.short_definition: Flavors that remain after swallowing wine.
terms.full_definition: Aftertaste is the flavor impression remaining after swallowing (or spitting) wine. A long, pleasant aftertaste indicates quality. The aftertaste may evolve, revealing different notes than the initial palate impression. Bitter or harsh aftertaste is considered a fault. Synonymous with finish and length.

terms.short_definition: Wine that has been matured over time.
terms.full_definition: Aged wine has been matured in barrel, tank, or bottle, developing complexity beyond its primary fruit character. Aging allows tannins to soften, colors to evolve, and tertiary aromas (leather, tobacco, earth) to develop. Not all wines improve with age; most are made for immediate consumption.

terms.short_definition: Wine with harsh tannins or acidity that dominates.
terms.full_definition: Aggressive describes wines with overly assertive tannins, acidity, or alcohol that overwhelm other components. Young wines may be aggressive but soften with age. Permanently aggressive wines are unbalanced. It's a negative descriptor indicating poor harmony, though some young wines intended for aging may show temporary aggression.

terms.short_definition: Spanish white grape from Rias Baixas, known for citrus and mineral character.
terms.full_definition: Albarino is a white grape variety primarily from Spain's Rias Baixas region in Galicia. It produces aromatic wines with citrus, peach, and saline minerality, often with notable texture. Traditionally grown on pergola trellises in granite soils. Also grown in Portugal (as Alvarinho) for Vinho Verde. Excellent with seafood.

terms.short_definition: Italian term meaning bitter, used for wines with bitter finish.
terms.full_definition: Amaro means 'bitter' in Italian and describes wines with noticeable bitterness on the finish. Some bitterness is characteristic of certain Italian reds (Barolo, Barbaresco) from Nebbiolo. When properly integrated, amaro adds complexity. Excessive bitterness is a fault, often from extracted seeds or unripe tannins.

terms.short_definition: Oak from North America with distinctive sweet, vanilla character.
terms.full_definition: American oak (Quercus alba) has wider grain than French oak, releasing flavor more quickly and intensely. It contributes pronounced vanilla, coconut, dill, and sweet spice notes. Less expensive than French oak, it's traditional for Rioja and widely used in Australia. Some consider it too aggressive for delicate wines.

terms.short_definition: Sherry style combining biological and oxidative aging.
terms.full_definition: Amontillado is a sherry style that begins aging under flor (biological aging) before continuing oxidatively when the flor dies or is killed by fortification. This produces wines with both the nutty complexity of biological aging and the amber color/dried fruit of oxidative aging. Color ranges from amber to mahogany.

terms.short_definition: Process occurring without oxygen.
terms.full_definition: Anaerobic describes processes occurring without oxygen. Alcoholic fermentation is anaerobic - yeast converts sugar to alcohol in the absence of oxygen. Carbonic maceration involves anaerobic intracellular fermentation. Reductive winemaking minimizes oxygen exposure. Anaerobic conditions prevent oxidation and acetobacter growth.

terms.short_definition: Wine with sharp, unintegrated components.
terms.full_definition: Angular describes wines with sharp, hard edges - acidity or tannin that juts out rather than flowing smoothly. Young wines may be angular but soften with age. Permanently angular wines lack integration and harmony. The opposite of round, smooth, or supple. Not necessarily negative for wines intended for aging.

terms.short_definition: Wine or drink served before a meal to stimulate appetite.
terms.full_definition: An aperitif is a drink served before a meal to stimulate appetite. Light, dry, refreshing wines work best: Champagne, dry sherry, white wines with good acidity. The word comes from Latin 'aperire' (to open). Traditional aperitif hour is an important social ritual in France, Italy, and Spain.

terms.short_definition: Wines with intensely fragrant characteristics.
terms.full_definition: Aromatic describes wines (and grape varieties) with pronounced, perfumed aromas. Aromatic varieties include Riesling, Gewurztraminer, Muscat, Torrontes, and Viognier. Their distinctive terpene compounds create intense floral and fruity scents. Aromatic wines often have moderate acidity and are best served young.

terms.short_definition: Wine flavored with herbs, spices, or botanicals.
terms.full_definition: Aromatized wines are flavored with herbs, spices, flowers, or other botanicals. Vermouth is the most famous example, flavored with wormwood and other aromatics. Quinquina contains quinine. These are typically fortified as well. Aromatized wines are used in cocktails and as aperitifs.

terms.short_definition: The direction a vineyard slope faces.
terms.full_definition: Aspect (or exposure) refers to the compass direction a vineyard slope faces. In the Northern Hemisphere, south-facing slopes receive more sun and warmth; in the Southern Hemisphere, north-facing is warmer. Aspect affects ripening, disease pressure, and wine style. Cooler aspects may be preferred in hot climates.

terms.short_definition: The initial impression when wine first enters the mouth.
terms.full_definition: Attack describes the first impression when wine enters the mouth - the initial burst of flavor and sensation before the mid-palate develops. A strong attack suggests intensity and concentration. Weak attack may indicate a dilute wine. Professional tasters evaluate attack, mid-palate, and finish separately.

terms.short_definition: Lean, restrained wine with firm structure.
terms.full_definition: Austere describes wines that are lean, restrained, and stripped-down rather than generous and opulent. Austerity can indicate age-worthy structure that will develop complexity, or it can signal insufficient ripeness or fruit. Traditional European styles are often more austere than fruit-forward New World wines.

terms.short_definition: Native or indigenous grape variety from a region.
terms.full_definition: Autochthonous describes grape varieties native to or originating in a particular region, as opposed to international varieties introduced from elsewhere. Italy has hundreds of autochthonous varieties (Nebbiolo, Sangiovese, Fiano). Interest in indigenous grapes has grown as regions seek distinctive identity beyond international varieties.

terms.short_definition: Breakdown of yeast cells adding complexity to wine.
terms.full_definition: Autolysis is the enzymatic breakdown of dead yeast cells during extended lees aging. It releases amino acids, mannoproteins, and other compounds that add body, creaminess, and bread/brioche aromas. Important in Champagne, quality sparkling wines, and sur lie whites. Extended autolysis can take years.

terms.short_definition: Wine developing slower than expected for its age.
terms.full_definition: Backward describes a wine that is less developed than expected for its age - it shows younger, tighter character than similar wines from the same vintage. Backward wines often have excellent aging potential but need more time. It's not a fault but requires patience. The opposite of forward or precocious.

terms.short_definition: Cooked, raisiny character from heat exposure.
terms.full_definition: Baked describes wines with a cooked, raisiny character, either from excessive heat during grape growing, poor storage (heat damage), or intentional winemaking (Madeira). In table wines, it typically indicates overripe grapes or heat damage and is considered a fault. In Madeira, baked character is intentional and desirable.

terms.short_definition: Resinous, herbal aromas like balsam or eucalyptus.
terms.full_definition: Balsamic describes resinous, aromatic characteristics like mint, eucalyptus, pine, or juniper - often found in aged Nebbiolo, Cabernet Sauvignon, or wines from warm Mediterranean climates where aromatic shrubs grow. Distinct from the Italian condiment. Can suggest either varietal character or garrigue influence.

terms.short_definition: DOCG for Nebbiolo wines from Piedmont, Italy.
terms.full_definition: Barbaresco is a DOCG in Piedmont, Italy, producing wines exclusively from Nebbiolo. Often called Barolo's sibling, it's typically more elegant and earlier-maturing than Barolo due to warmer, lower-elevation vineyards. Famous producers include Gaja, Produttori del Barbaresco, and Bruno Giacosa. The commune and two others (Neive, Treiso) comprise the zone.

terms.short_definition: Prestigious DOCG for Nebbiolo wines from Piedmont.
terms.full_definition: Barolo is a DOCG in Piedmont, Italy, considered one of Italy's greatest red wines. Made exclusively from Nebbiolo, it's known for powerful tannins, high acidity, and complex aromas of tar, roses, and dried herbs. Requires minimum 38 months aging (62 for Riserva). Famous crus include Cannubi, Brunate, and Vigna Rionda.

terms.short_definition: White grape from Southwest France for Tursan wines.
terms.full_definition: Baroque is a white grape variety indigenous to Southwest France, primarily Tursan appellation. It produces wines with floral aromatics and moderate acidity. Nearly extinct by the 1970s, it has been revived as part of efforts to preserve local grape heritage. Often blended with other varieties.

terms.short_definition: Wine fermented in oak barrels rather than tanks.
terms.full_definition: Barrel fermentation conducts primary fermentation in oak barrels rather than stainless steel tanks. This integrates oak character more seamlessly, often producing wines with subtle oak influence and creamy texture. Common for premium Chardonnay and some reds. The technique requires more labor and barrel inventory than tank fermentation.

terms.short_definition: Farm-like aromas, often from Brettanomyces.
terms.full_definition: Barnyard describes animal, farm-like aromas - hay, manure, horse blanket, leather - typically caused by Brettanomyces yeast. At low levels, some consider it adds complexity; at high levels, it's a fault. Traditionally accepted in some Rhone and Bordeaux wines but increasingly rejected by modern palates.

terms.short_definition: Still wine used as foundation for sparkling wine.
terms.full_definition: Base wine is the still wine used to produce sparkling wine before secondary fermentation adds bubbles. For Champagne, base wine is typically high acid, low alcohol, and relatively neutral. Multiple base wines may be blended from different varieties, vineyards, and vintages to create the final cuvee.

terms.short_definition: French region known for fruity Gamay-based wines.
terms.full_definition: Beaujolais is a French wine region producing red wines from Gamay grapes. Styles range from simple, carbonic-macerated Beaujolais and Beaujolais-Villages to serious, age-worthy wines from the ten Crus (Morgon, Moulin-a-Vent, Fleurie, etc.). Beaujolais Nouveau, released annually in November, is famously fresh and fruity.

terms.short_definition: Clay used for fining wine to remove proteins.
terms.full_definition: Bentonite is a type of clay used as a fining agent to remove proteins that can cause haze in white wines. It carries a negative charge that attracts positively charged proteins, causing them to precipitate. Bentonite can strip some color and flavor if used excessively. It's one of the most common fining agents.

terms.short_definition: Wine with powerful, concentrated character.
terms.full_definition: Big describes wines with substantial body, high alcohol, intense flavors, and pronounced tannins or extract. Big wines make bold statements and pair with rich foods. The term doesn't necessarily indicate quality - balance matters. Big wines contrast with elegant, delicate, or restrained styles.

terms.short_definition: One of the basic tastes, detected at the back of the tongue.
terms.full_definition: Bitter is one of five basic tastes, detected primarily at the back of the tongue. In wine, bitterness comes from tannins, particularly from grape seeds and stems or excessive oak extraction. Some bitterness is characteristic of certain varieties (Nebbiolo) and styles. Excessive bitterness is a fault.

terms.short_definition: Aromas and flavors of dark-colored fruits.
terms.full_definition: Black fruit refers to aromas and flavors of dark-colored fruits: blackberry, black cherry, black currant (cassis), blackcurrant, and plum. Common in full-bodied red wines, particularly Cabernet Sauvignon, Syrah, and Malbec. Contrasts with red fruit (strawberry, cherry, raspberry) typically found in lighter wines.

terms.short_definition: Tasting wine without knowing its identity.
terms.full_definition: Blind tasting evaluates wine without knowing its identity - bottles may be wrapped, in bags, or decanted into neutral glasses. This eliminates bias from reputation, price, or label. Single-blind conceals producer; double-blind conceals variety, region, and vintage. Professional certifications require blind tasting skills.

terms.short_definition: American term for light-colored rose, often sweet.
terms.full_definition: Blush is an American term for pink wine, popularized by White Zinfandel in the 1980s. It often implies a sweeter, simpler style than rose. The term distinguished American pink wines from drier French roses. Usage has declined as dry rose gained popularity, though sweet blush wines remain popular.

terms.short_definition: Spanish term for winery or wine cellar.
terms.full_definition: Bodega is the Spanish word for winery, wine cellar, or wine storage facility. It appears in many Spanish winery names (Bodegas Torres, Bodegas Muga). In some contexts, it refers specifically to the aging cellar. The Portuguese equivalent is adega.

terms.short_definition: Wine made from grapes affected by noble rot.
terms.full_definition: Botrytized wines are made from grapes affected by Botrytis cinerea (noble rot), which concentrates sugars and adds distinctive honeyed, marmalade character. Examples include Sauternes, Tokaji, and TBA/BA German wines. The fungus is labor-intensive to manage and only forms in specific microclimates.

terms.short_definition: Austrian white grape used for sweet wines.
terms.full_definition: Bouvier is a white grape variety grown primarily in Austria and occasionally Slovenia. It produces early-ripening, low-acid grapes susceptible to botrytis, making it suitable for sweet wines. Its neutral character makes it less interesting for dry wines. Named after breeder Clotar Bouvier.

terms.short_definition: Sparkling wine with zero dosage added.
terms.full_definition: Brut Nature (also Zero Dosage, Non-Dose, or Brut Zero) is sparkling wine bottled without adding any sugar after disgorgement. Residual sugar must be less than 3 g/L. This style showcases terroir and base wine purity but requires ripe, balanced fruit since dosage can't mask flaws. Growing in popularity.

terms.short_definition: Free-standing vine trained without trellising.
terms.full_definition: Bush vine (gobelet, head-trained) is a vine training system where each plant stands independently without wire support. Traditional in hot, dry regions (Southern Rhone, Mediterranean, old California), the bush shape shades fruit from intense sun. Bush vines require hand labor and are often old, low-yielding, and prized for concentrated wines.

terms.short_definition: Red grape parent of Cabernet Sauvignon, known for herbal notes.
terms.full_definition: Cabernet Franc is a red grape variety, one of the parents of Cabernet Sauvignon (with Sauvignon Blanc). It produces wines with red fruit, violets, and herbal/bell pepper notes. A blending component in Bordeaux, it stars solo in Loire Valley (Chinon, Bourgueil). Grows well in cooler climates than Cabernet Sauvignon.

terms.short_definition: The world's most famous red grape, known for cassis and structure.
terms.full_definition: Cabernet Sauvignon is the most planted premium red grape worldwide, originating from a Cabernet Franc x Sauvignon Blanc crossing in Bordeaux. It produces structured, age-worthy wines with black currant, cedar, and green pepper notes. Thick skins create deep color and firm tannins. Successful from Bordeaux to Napa to Australia.

terms.short_definition: Italian term for winery or cellar.
terms.full_definition: Cantina is the Italian word for winery, wine cellar, or wine cooperative. 'Cantina sociale' indicates a cooperative winery. The term appears in many Italian producer names. Similar to French cave or Spanish bodega.

terms.short_definition: The layer of skins that floats during red wine fermentation.
terms.full_definition: The cap is the layer of grape skins, seeds, and solids that floats to the surface during red wine fermentation, pushed up by CO2. Cap management (punch-down, pump-over, rack-and-return) keeps the cap moist and extracts color, tannin, and flavor. Without management, the cap can dry out and develop problems.

terms.short_definition: High-yielding red grape from Mediterranean regions.
terms.full_definition: Carignan (Carignane, Carinena, Mazuelo) is a red grape variety widely planted in Mediterranean regions. Often high-yielding and rustic, old-vine Carignan from quality sites produces concentrated, tannic wines with dark fruit and spice. Important in Languedoc blends and old-vine California wines. Requires warm, dry conditions.

terms.short_definition: Chilean red grape, formerly confused with Merlot.
terms.full_definition: Carmenere is a red grape variety originally from Bordeaux, now primarily grown in Chile. Long thought to be Merlot, its identity was confirmed via DNA in 1994. It produces wines with dark fruit, herbal notes, and green pepper character when underripe. Chile has made it a signature variety.

terms.short_definition: Milk protein used for fining wine.
terms.full_definition: Casein is a milk protein used as a fining agent to remove brown color and harsh phenolics from white wines. It can soften astringency and reduce bitterness. Not suitable for vegan wines. Must be declared as allergen in some countries. One of several protein-based fining agents.

terms.short_definition: Black currant flavor, characteristic of Cabernet Sauvignon.
terms.full_definition: Cassis is the French word for black currant, and a classic descriptor for Cabernet Sauvignon. The dark, concentrated berry flavor is considered varietal character for quality Cabernet. Cassis is also a French AOC for Provence wines and a blackcurrant liqueur used in Kir cocktails.

terms.short_definition: French term for wine cellar, usually underground.
terms.full_definition: Cave is the French word for cellar, typically referring to underground wine storage. 'Cave cooperative' is a cooperative winery. Temperature-stable caves are ideal for wine aging. The term appears in many French producer names. Compare with chai (above-ground cellar, particularly in Bordeaux).

terms.short_definition: Aromatic wood note found in aged Cabernet and oak-aged wines.
terms.full_definition: Cedar describes an aromatic, pencil-shaving character common in Bordeaux, Cabernet Sauvignon, and wines aged in quality oak. It's part of the 'cigar box' aromatic complex that develops with age. Cedar notes are generally positive, suggesting complexity and quality aging. The scent comes from cedrol compounds.

terms.short_definition: French term for grape variety or blend composition.
terms.full_definition: Cepage is the French term for grape variety. It also refers to the blend composition of a wine - the percentages of different varieties. 'Encepagement' describes the varietal mix planted in a vineyard. Understanding cepage helps decode French labels that often don't state variety explicitly.

terms.short_definition: Northern Burgundy region for mineral, unoaked Chardonnay.
terms.full_definition: Chablis is a Burgundy appellation in Yonne, producing white wines exclusively from Chardonnay. Known for steely minerality, high acidity, and traditionally unoaked style. The cool climate and Kimmeridgian limestone soils create distinctive wines. Hierarchy includes Petit Chablis, Chablis, Premier Cru, and Grand Cru.

terms.short_definition: Dry, powdery tannin texture or mineral character.
terms.full_definition: Chalky describes either a fine, powdery tannin texture or mineral character evoking chalk soil. Chalky tannins are fine-grained and drying but not harsh. Chalky minerality suggests wines from chalk-based terroirs like Champagne or Jerez. Both usages are generally positive descriptors.

terms.short_definition: World's most popular white grape, styles range from crisp to rich.
terms.full_definition: Chardonnay is the most planted white wine grape globally, originating in Burgundy. It's remarkably versatile - styles range from lean, mineral Chablis to rich, oaky California versions. The grape is neutral, reflecting terroir and winemaking more than varietal character. Essential for Champagne and many sparkling wines.

terms.short_definition: Versatile Loire white grape producing dry to sweet wines.
terms.full_definition: Chenin Blanc is a white grape variety originating in Loire Valley, producing wines in every style from bone-dry to lusciously sweet, still to sparkling. High acidity enables excellent aging. Famous Loire appellations include Vouvray, Savennieres, and sweet Coteaux du Layon. South Africa is now the largest producer.

terms.short_definition: Rich, dark, cocoa-like flavor in full-bodied red wines.
terms.full_definition: Chocolate describes rich, cocoa-like flavors found in full-bodied red wines, often from warm climates or heavily toasted oak barrels. Dark chocolate notes are common in ripe Merlot, Shiraz, and Zinfandel. The character comes from various compounds including lactones and volatile phenols. Generally a positive descriptor.

terms.short_definition: Complex cedar, tobacco, and spice aromas in aged wines.
terms.full_definition: Cigar box describes an aromatic complex combining cedar, tobacco, earth, and spice, often developing in aged Bordeaux and quality Cabernet. It evokes the scent of an opened box of fine cigars. This tertiary character indicates successful bottle aging and quality wine. Among the most evocative wine descriptors.

terms.short_definition: Lemon, lime, grapefruit, or orange aromas and flavors.
terms.full_definition: Citrus describes aromas and flavors of lemons, limes, grapefruit, and oranges - common in white wines, especially Sauvignon Blanc, Riesling, and wines from cool climates. Citrus character comes from terpene compounds. It's associated with freshness, youth, and vibrant acidity.

terms.short_definition: Wine showing typical character for its type.
terms.full_definition: Classic describes a wine that exemplifies its type - a classic Burgundy shows typical Burgundy character. It implies traditional, benchmark quality rather than innovation. Classic wines are often used as teaching examples. Not all excellent wines are classic; some great wines are deliberately atypical.

terms.short_definition: Wine without faults or off-flavors.
terms.full_definition: Clean describes wine free of faults, off-flavors, or unwanted characteristics. A clean wine shows pure fruit and intended character without contamination. It's a baseline quality requirement rather than a compliment. The opposite would be funky, dirty, or faulty.

terms.short_definition: Wine with visible particles or haze.
terms.full_definition: Cloudy describes wine with visible suspended particles or lack of clarity. In conventional wine, cloudiness indicates a fault (protein instability, refermentation, or contamination). However, some natural wines are intentionally cloudy from minimal fining/filtration. Context determines if it's a fault or style choice.

terms.short_definition: Excessively sweet, lacking balancing acidity.
terms.full_definition: Cloying describes wine that is excessively sweet, heavy, and sticky, lacking the acidity to balance sweetness. It's a fault in any sweet wine - well-made dessert wines have sufficient acidity to remain refreshing. Cloying wines fatigue the palate quickly.

terms.short_definition: Rough, unrefined texture or flavors.
terms.full_definition: Coarse describes rough, grainy, unrefined tannins or texture - lacking polish and elegance. It may indicate underripe tannins, harsh extraction, or poor quality fruit. Some coarse wines soften with age; others remain rough. The opposite of silky, fine, or elegant.

terms.short_definition: Roasted, espresso-like aromas from oak or aged wine.
terms.full_definition: Coffee or espresso notes appear in wines aged in heavily toasted oak barrels or in mature wines. The roasted, bitter-sweet coffee character comes from wood pyrolysis during barrel toasting. Common in heavily oaked reds and some aged wines. Related to mocha and chocolate notes.

terms.short_definition: Chilling wine to precipitate tartrate crystals.
terms.full_definition: Cold stabilization chills wine to near-freezing temperatures, causing tartrate crystals to form and precipitate before bottling. This prevents crystal formation in the bottle, which some consumers mistake for flaws. Some premium producers skip this step, preferring minimal intervention. Crystals are harmless.

terms.short_definition: A French village or municipality with wine designation.
terms.full_definition: Commune is a French administrative term for a village or municipality. In wine, it refers to villages with their own appellation (Pauillac, Meursault). Commune-level wines represent a step up from regional wines, showing more specific terroir character. Multiple communes may share an appellation.

terms.short_definition: Climate with hot summers, cold winters, and large temperature swings.
terms.full_definition: Continental climate features hot summers, cold winters, and significant diurnal temperature variation. Wine regions include Burgundy, Champagne, Alsace, and parts of Oregon and Washington. The temperature swings help develop flavor complexity while retaining acidity. Continental regions face frost risk and vintage variation.

terms.short_definition: Winery owned collectively by grape growers.
terms.full_definition: A cooperativa (cooperative, cantina sociale) is a winery collectively owned by member grape growers who share equipment, facilities, and marketing. Quality varies widely - some cooperatives produce excellent wines at value prices; others focus on volume. Important in regions with small landholdings like Alsace, Trentino, and Languedoc.

terms.short_definition: Poor fruit set resulting in low yields.
terms.full_definition: Coulure is a vine disorder where flowers fail to set into berries, reducing yields significantly. It's caused by poor weather (rain, cold, wind) during flowering, or vine imbalances. Some varieties like Merlot and Malbec are particularly susceptible. While reducing quantity, it can concentrate remaining fruit quality.

terms.short_definition: Simple, everyday wine for immediate consumption.
terms.full_definition: Country wine refers to simple, unpretentious wines meant for everyday drinking rather than cellaring. Historically, it denoted non-appellation wines in European classification. The category emphasizes value, drinkability, and local character over complexity or aging potential. French 'vin de pays' is a formalized version.

terms.short_definition: Rich, smooth texture suggesting dairy cream.
terms.full_definition: Creamy describes a rich, smooth texture resembling dairy cream. It results from malolactic fermentation (producing diacetyl), lees contact, or barrel fermentation. Common descriptor for oaked Chardonnay and quality sparkling wines. Can be positive or excessive depending on context and preference.

terms.short_definition: Grape variety bred from two varieties of the same species.
terms.full_definition: A crossing is a grape variety created by breeding two varieties of the same species (typically Vitis vinifera). This differs from hybrids, which cross different species. Famous crossings include Muller-Thurgau (Riesling x Madeleine Royale), Pinotage (Pinot Noir x Cinsaut), and Scheurebe (Riesling x unknown).

terms.short_definition: Bordeaux classification tier below cru classe.
terms.full_definition: Cru Bourgeois is a Bordeaux classification for chateaux not included in the 1855 Classification, covering estates in the Medoc. The classification has been controversial, disbanded, and reinstated multiple times. Currently, it includes three tiers: Cru Bourgeois, Cru Bourgeois Superieur, and Cru Bourgeois Exceptionnel.

terms.short_definition: A classified growth estate in Bordeaux or other regions.
terms.full_definition: Cru classe ('classified growth') refers to estates included in official classifications. In Bordeaux, the 1855 Classification ranked Left Bank estates; separate classifications exist for Graves, Saint-Emilion, and Cru Bourgeois. The designation implies historical quality reputation but doesn't guarantee current excellence.

terms.short_definition: Highly sought-after wine with limited production and high prices.
terms.full_definition: Cult wine describes highly coveted wines with tiny production, mailing list allocation, and premium prices. The term emerged in 1990s California for wines like Screaming Eagle, Harlan, and Sine Qua Non. Cult status often depends more on scarcity and critical acclaim than objective quality. Price premiums can be extreme.

terms.short_definition: Tank method for sparkling wine production.
terms.full_definition: Cuve close (Charmat method, tank method) produces sparkling wine through secondary fermentation in large pressurized tanks rather than individual bottles. It's faster and more economical than traditional method, preserving primary fruit character. Used for Prosecco, Lambrusco, and value sparkling wines. Produces lighter, fruitier bubbles.

terms.short_definition: Deep color or flavors of dark fruits and chocolate.
terms.full_definition: Dark describes either visual color (deep ruby, purple, opaque) or flavor profile (dark fruits, chocolate, coffee, earth). Dark wines typically come from thick-skinned grapes, warm climates, or extended maceration. The opposite of light, pale, or bright. Context determines specific meaning.

terms.short_definition: Reducing acid levels in wine.
terms.full_definition: Deacidification lowers acid levels in wine, typically using potassium bicarbonate, calcium carbonate, or ion exchange. It's practiced in cool climates where grapes can become excessively acidic. Malolactic fermentation is a natural form of deacidification. Excessive chemical deacidification can leave wine flat and tired.

terms.short_definition: Concentrated color or layered, profound flavors.
terms.full_definition: Deep describes either concentrated color (difficult to see through) or layered, profound flavors with great depth. Deep wines show complexity and substance. Deep color often indicates extended maceration, thick-skinned grapes, or youth. Deep flavors suggest quality and concentration.

terms.short_definition: Subtle, refined wine with light body.
terms.full_definition: Delicate describes wines with subtle, fine, understated character rather than power or intensity. Delicate wines require attention to appreciate their nuance. Champagne, Burgundy, Riesling, and Pinot Noir often show delicacy. The opposite of powerful, bold, or big. A positive quality when intentional.

terms.short_definition: Concentrated, compact flavors and texture.
terms.full_definition: Dense describes wines with tightly packed, concentrated flavors and substantial texture. Dense wines feel weighty and substantial without being heavy. The character suggests quality extraction, ripe fruit, and good concentration. Dense wines often age well as components integrate.

terms.short_definition: Sweet wine typically served with or as dessert.
terms.full_definition: Dessert wines are sweet wines with high residual sugar, served with or as dessert. Production methods include late harvest, botrytis infection, drying grapes (passito), fortification, or freezing (ice wine). Famous examples include Sauternes, Tokaji, Vin Santo, and Port. Sugar levels range from moderately sweet to intensely sweet.

terms.short_definition: Compound creating buttery, creamy aromas.
terms.full_definition: Diacetyl is a chemical compound produced during malolactic fermentation that creates buttery, butterscotch, or creamy aromas. Common in oaked Chardonnay. At low levels it adds richness; excessive amounts can seem artificial. Some winemakers encourage it, others minimize it through technique.

terms.short_definition: Off-putting earthy or barnyard aromas suggesting contamination.
terms.full_definition: Dirty describes wines with unpleasant earthy, barnyard, or musty aromas that suggest poor hygiene, brett contamination, or cellar problems. Distinguished from positive earthiness, dirty implies something went wrong. Often caused by brettanomyces, dirty barrels, or poor sanitation.

terms.short_definition: Denominacion de Origen - Spanish wine appellation system.
terms.full_definition: DO (Denominacion de Origen) is Spain's regulated wine appellation system, equivalent to French AOC. It defines geographic boundaries, permitted grapes, yields, and winemaking practices. Higher tiers include DOCa/DOQ (Rioja, Priorat) and Vino de Pago for single estates. Below DO are IGP and table wine categories.

terms.short_definition: Italian term for sweet wine.
terms.full_definition: Dolce is the Italian word for sweet, used on labels to indicate sweet wines. Italian sweetness levels progress from secco (dry) through abboccato (off-dry), amabile (semi-sweet), to dolce (sweet). Often seen with Asti Spumante, Moscato, and Recioto wines.

terms.short_definition: French term meaning sweet; sweetest level in sparkling wine.
terms.full_definition: Doux means sweet in French. For Champagne and sparkling wines, it's the sweetest classification with over 50 grams per liter of residual sugar. For still wines, doux or moelleux indicates a sweet style. Rarely seen in modern Champagne, more common historically.

terms.short_definition: Flavors of raisins, figs, prunes, or dates in wine.
terms.full_definition: Dried fruit describes flavors of raisins, figs, prunes, dates, or dried apricots. Often found in sweet wines, fortified wines, aged reds, or wines from hot climates. Can result from dried grape production (passito, Amarone), botrytis, or bottle age. Positive in appropriate styles.

terms.short_definition: Dry, mineral texture suggesting dusty roads or old cellars.
terms.full_definition: Dusty describes a dry, chalky, or mineral texture reminiscent of dusty roads or old cellars. Often associated with aged wines, certain terroirs, or specific grape varieties like Nebbiolo. Can also refer to fine-grained tannins. Generally positive, adding complexity and sense of place.

terms.short_definition: Clear fruit brandy or distilled spirits.
terms.full_definition: Eaux-de-vie ('water of life') are clear, unaged fruit brandies distilled from fermented fruit. While not wine, they're related to wine production through grape-based varieties. Used in fortification (neutral spirits) and as digestifs. Examples include kirsch (cherry), framboise (raspberry), and marc (grape pomace).

terms.short_definition: Having bubbles from dissolved carbon dioxide.
terms.full_definition: Effervescent describes wine with visible bubbles from dissolved carbon dioxide. Includes fully sparkling wines (Champagne, Cava), lightly sparkling (frizzante, petillant), and slightly bubbly wines. Effervescence can be intentional or an unintentional fault in still wine.

terms.short_definition: Traditional fining agent for clarifying red wine.
terms.full_definition: Egg whites (albumen) are a traditional fining agent used primarily for red wines. The protein binds with harsh tannins and particles, which settle out of the wine. Typically 2-4 egg whites per barrel are used. Results in softer, more polished wine. Increasingly replaced by vegan alternatives.

terms.short_definition: Graceful, balanced wine with finesse rather than power.
terms.full_definition: Elegant describes wines showing grace, balance, and refinement rather than power or concentration. Elegant wines are harmonious, with integrated components and subtle complexity. Often applied to Burgundy, Barolo, and Champagne. The opposite of rustic, heavy, or clumsy. High praise implying sophistication.

terms.short_definition: The blend of grape varieties planted in a vineyard.
terms.full_definition: Encepagement refers to the mix of grape varieties planted in a vineyard or used in a wine blend. In regions like Chateauneuf-du-Pape with many permitted varieties, encepagement decisions shape wine style. The term encompasses both vineyard composition and the final blend proportions.

terms.short_definition: A wine scientist or professionally trained winemaker.
terms.full_definition: An enologist (oenologist) is a professional with formal training in wine science, typically holding a degree in enology. They understand fermentation, chemistry, microbiology, and cellar practices at a technical level. Distinguished from winemaker by academic credentials, though roles overlap. The science is called enology.

terms.short_definition: Wine made from grapes grown on property owned or controlled by the winery.
terms.full_definition: Estate-bottled wine comes from grapes grown in vineyards owned or controlled by the winery, fermented and bottled on the property. In the US, both vineyards and winery must be in the same appellation. Estate implies quality control from vine to bottle, though doesn't guarantee quality.

terms.short_definition: The alcohol in wine produced by yeast fermentation.
terms.full_definition: Ethanol (ethyl alcohol) is the drinkable alcohol in wine, produced when yeast consumes grape sugars during fermentation. Expressed as percentage by volume (ABV), wine ranges from 5% to 15%+ naturally, with fortified wines reaching 20%+. Ethanol contributes body, warmth, sweetness perception, and preservation.

terms.short_definition: Very dry sparkling wine with minimal dosage.
terms.full_definition: Extra Brut is a sparkling wine sweetness category between Brut Nature and Brut, containing 0-6 grams per liter of residual sugar. Drier than standard Brut (up to 12 g/L), it shows more austere, mineral character with pure expression of base wine and terroir. Increasingly popular with quality Champagne.

terms.short_definition: Drawing color, tannin, and flavor from grape skins during winemaking.
terms.full_definition: Extraction is the process of drawing color, tannins, and flavor compounds from grape skins, seeds, and sometimes stems during maceration and fermentation. Techniques include cold soaking, punch downs, pump overs, extended maceration, and pressing decisions. Over-extraction produces harsh, bitter wines; under-extraction yields thin wines.

terms.short_definition: Full-bodied, rich wine often lacking sufficient acidity.
terms.full_definition: Fat describes full-bodied, unctuous wines with rich texture, often from ripe vintages or warm climates. While implying richness, fat often suggests insufficient acidity to provide balance and freshness. A wine can be positively 'rich' or negatively 'fat' depending on whether it maintains tension.

terms.short_definition: Removing particles from wine by passing through filtering media.
terms.full_definition: Filtration removes suspended particles, yeast, and bacteria from wine by passing it through filter media of various porosity. Methods include pad filtration, membrane filtration, and cross-flow filtration. Sterile filtration before bottling prevents refermentation. Unfiltered wines may have more texture but risk instability.

terms.short_definition: High-quality wine, often collectible with aging potential.
terms.full_definition: Fine wine refers to high-quality wines with aging potential, typically from prestigious regions with established track records. The term has no legal definition but generally implies wines above $50-100 that merit cellaring. The fine wine market includes Bordeaux, Burgundy, top Italian and California producers, and increasingly other regions.

terms.short_definition: Elegance, subtlety, and delicate precision in wine.
terms.full_definition: Finesse describes wines showing elegance, subtlety, and refined precision rather than power or weight. A wine with finesse has delicate complexity, fine-grained textures, and seamless balance. The term implies quality and sophistication. Often applied to Burgundy, Champagne, and top Riesling.

terms.short_definition: The overall combination of taste characteristics in a wine.
terms.full_definition: Flavor profile is the complete picture of a wine's taste characteristics, including fruit character, savory notes, structure (acidity, tannin, alcohol), texture, and finish. Understanding flavor profiles helps match wine with food and identify personal preferences. Profiles vary by grape, region, vintage, and winemaking.

terms.short_definition: Wine with ripe, soft, substantial fruit character.
terms.full_definition: Fleshy describes wines with soft, ripe, substantial fruit character and generous texture. Implies plump, mouth-filling fruit without hardness or austerity. Usually positive, suggesting quality and ripeness. Common in warm climate wines, ripe vintages, and fruit-forward styles. The opposite of lean or austere.

terms.short_definition: Aromas reminiscent of flowers.
terms.full_definition: Floral describes wine aromas suggesting flowers such as rose, violet, jasmine, orange blossom, honeysuckle, or elderflower. Common in aromatic whites (Riesling, Gewurztraminer, Muscat) and certain reds (Nebbiolo for rose/violet, Gamay). Derived from grape terpenes and fermentation compounds. Generally positive and attractive.

terms.short_definition: Pronounced floral aromatics, sometimes excessively so.
terms.full_definition: Flowery describes wines with pronounced flower aromas, similar to but often more intense than 'floral.' Can suggest excessive perfume if overdone. Found in aromatic varieties like Muscat, Gewurztraminer, and Torrontes. Context determines if flowery is positive (expressive) or negative (cloying).

terms.short_definition: Wine that excels with food rather than on its own.
terms.full_definition: Food wine describes wines that shine at the table rather than as standalone sippers. They typically have higher acidity, moderate alcohol, and restrained fruit, providing refreshment and complement to cuisine rather than demanding attention. Many Italian, French, and Old World wines are classic food wines.

terms.short_definition: Wine showing flavors upfront and approachably.
terms.full_definition: Forward describes wines that present their flavors openly and immediately, without requiring time or effort to reveal character. Forward wines are approachable young and express fruit prominently. Can be positive (generous, friendly) or suggest lack of depth. The opposite of closed, backward, or reserved.

terms.short_definition: Distinctive musky aroma of native American grape species.
terms.full_definition: Foxy describes the distinctive musky, grapey aroma found in wines from Vitis labrusca and other native American grape species (Concord, Catawba, Niagara). The compound methyl anthranilate creates this candy-grape character. Considered a fault in European wines but traditional in Eastern American varieties.

terms.short_definition: Displaying distinct fruit aromas and flavors.
terms.full_definition: Fruity describes wines with prominent fruit character, whether fresh, dried, or cooked. Different from fruit-forward intensity, fruity simply indicates fruit presence. Fruit types include citrus, tropical, stone fruit, berries, and orchard fruit. Primary fruity character often fades as wines develop secondary and tertiary notes.

terms.short_definition: Purchasing wine before release while still in barrel.
terms.full_definition: Wine futures involve purchasing wine while still in barrel, paying current prices for future delivery. Most associated with Bordeaux en primeur but practiced elsewhere. Benefits theoretically include access to sought-after wines and price advantages, though modern pricing often negates savings.

terms.short_definition: Portuguese term for wines with extended aging.
terms.full_definition: Garrafeira is a Portuguese designation indicating extended aging: reds must age 2+ years in barrel and 1+ year in bottle; whites need 6 months in barrel and 6 months in bottle. Similar concept to Spanish Reserva. The term also refers to a wine shop or cellar.

terms.short_definition: Animal-derived fining agent for softening wines.
terms.full_definition: Gelatine is a protein fining agent derived from animal collagen, used to soften harsh tannins and clarify wine. Different types suit different wine styles. More aggressive than egg whites but effective for astringent wines. Not vegan; increasingly replaced by plant-based alternatives.

terms.short_definition: Basic wine sold by style rather than variety or origin.
terms.full_definition: Generic wine is sold by general type (red, white, rose) or borrowed regional names rather than grape variety or specific origin. Historically included 'California Burgundy' or 'American Champagne.' These misleading terms are now restricted. Also refers to basic, entry-level wine without distinctive character.

terms.short_definition: Wine giving ample fruit, flavor, and pleasure.
terms.full_definition: Generous describes wines that give freely of their fruit, flavor, and pleasure. A generous wine feels abundant and satisfying without being excessive. Usually positive, suggesting quality and approachability. Can contrast with more austere, restrained, or intellectual wine styles.

terms.short_definition: Joining vine scion to rootstock from different species.
terms.full_definition: Grafting joins the fruiting portion (scion) of a Vitis vinifera vine to rootstock from phylloxera-resistant American vine species. Necessary since the phylloxera epidemic devastated European vineyards in the late 1800s. Different rootstocks offer various characteristics: drought tolerance, vigor control, and soil adaptation.

terms.short_definition: A Bordeaux chateau's primary, top-tier wine.
terms.full_definition: Grand Vin ('great wine') refers to a Bordeaux estate's primary, flagship wine, as opposed to the second wine. It receives the best barrel selection and carries the chateau name directly. The term implies highest quality from each vintage, though has no legal definition outside Bordeaux.

terms.short_definition: Fresh, green, herbaceous character like cut grass.
terms.full_definition: Grassy describes aromas and flavors of fresh cut grass, green herbs, or meadow. Found in Sauvignon Blanc, Gruner Veltliner, and underripe wines.
