Caused by methoxypyrazines, compounds also creating bell pepper notes. Can be positive (fresh, herbal) or negative (underripe, vegetal) depending on intensity.

terms.short_definition: Moving wine by gravity rather than pumps.
terms.full_definition: Gravity flow uses gravity instead of mechanical pumps to move wine through the winery, typically in multi-level facilities where grapes enter at top and wine flows down through processing stages. Proponents claim gentler handling preserves quality. Modern gravity-flow wineries are architecturally ambitious.

terms.short_definition: Wine of exceptional quality, balance, and character.
terms.full_definition: Great wine transcends good wine through exceptional balance, complexity, depth, and character. Subjective but recognized by consensus among critics and collectors. Great wines typically age well, express terroir, and provide memorable drinking experiences. Not defined by price alone, though correlation exists.

terms.short_definition: Unripe, herbaceous character from underripe grapes.
terms.full_definition: Green describes wines showing unripe, herbaceous, or vegetal character from underripe grapes. Flavors include bell pepper, grass, green tomato, and bitter herbs. In excess, green character is a fault suggesting poor viticultural year or premature harvest. Subtle green notes can add freshness.

terms.short_definition: German term for medium-dry or off-dry wine.
terms.full_definition: Halbtrocken ('half-dry') is a German sweetness designation for wines with 9-18 grams per liter residual sugar (with restrictions based on acidity). The style is between trocken (dry) and lieblich (semi-sweet). Off-dry Riesling balances residual sugar with acidity for food-friendly character.

terms.short_definition: Duration grapes remain on vine before harvest.
terms.full_definition: Hang time is the period between veraison and harvest when grapes hang on the vine ripening. Extended hang time allows greater phenolic development, sugar accumulation, and flavor concentration. However, excessive hang time risks overripeness, high alcohol, and loss of acidity. Optimal hang time varies by variety and style goals.

terms.short_definition: Unyielding wine with aggressive tannins or acidity.
terms.full_definition: Hard describes wines with aggressive, unyielding tannins or acidity that dominate the palate. Young tannic reds often seem hard before softening with age. The term can indicate wine needing time (positive) or structural imbalance (negative). Context and wine style determine interpretation.

terms.short_definition: Wine with well-integrated, balanced components.
terms.full_definition: Harmonious describes wines where all components work together seamlessly. No single element dominates; fruit, acid, tannin, oak, and alcohol integrate into a unified whole. Harmony typically develops with bottle age as wines evolve. A key indicator of quality and balance.

terms.short_definition: Unpleasantly aggressive tannins, acid, or alcohol.
terms.full_definition: Harsh describes unpleasantly aggressive sensations from excessive tannin, acid, or alcohol. Unlike hard wines that may soften, harsh wines suggest fundamental imbalance. Often results from underripe grapes, over-extraction, or poor winemaking. Distinguished from firm or gripping by its unpleasant nature.

terms.short_definition: Wine lacking clarity, appearing cloudy.
terms.full_definition: Hazy describes wines lacking visual clarity, appearing cloudy or turbid. Can indicate fault (bacterial contamination, protein instability) or intentional style (unfiltered, natural wine). Context matters: hazy is problematic in conventionally made wine but acceptable in natural wines. Distinguished from brilliant or clear.

terms.short_definition: Bush vine training without trellis support.
terms.full_definition: Head-trained vines (bush vines, gobelet) grow as freestanding bushes without trellis support, with spurs arranged around a central head. Traditional in Mediterranean regions, particularly for old vines. The method suits hot, dry climates where shade protects fruit. More labor-intensive but associated with quality old vines.

terms.short_definition: Wine spoilage from exposure to high temperatures.
terms.full_definition: Heat damage occurs when wine is exposed to high temperatures (above 75-80F) causing premature aging, cooked flavors, and oxidation. Signs include pushed-out corks, leakage, and stewed fruit character. Proper storage at 55-60F is essential. Heat damage is irreversible and a significant quality fault.

terms.short_definition: Overly full wine lacking freshness and lift.
terms.full_definition: Heavy describes wines that feel ponderous, overly full, and lacking freshness or lift. Usually negative, suggesting excess alcohol, extract, or oak without balancing acidity. Heavy wines tire the palate quickly. Distinguished from full-bodied (positive) by lack of elegance and refreshment.

terms.short_definition: Metric land measurement equal to 2.47 acres.
terms.full_definition: A hectare (ha) equals 10,000 square meters or approximately 2.47 acres. Standard land measurement for vineyards in metric countries. Wine production often reported as hectoliters per hectare (hl/ha). A typical quality-focused vineyard might yield 30-50 hl/ha.

terms.short_definition: Volume measurement equal to 100 liters.
terms.full_definition: A hectoliter (hl) equals 100 liters, approximately 133 standard bottles or 26.4 gallons. Used to measure wine production and yields. Yields expressed as hectoliters per hectare (hl/ha) indicate vineyard productivity. Quality appellations often limit yields to 45-55 hl/ha.

terms.short_definition: Aromas of dried or fresh herbs.
terms.full_definition: Herbal describes aromas of dried or fresh herbs including thyme, rosemary, sage, oregano, lavender, and mint. Common in Southern French wines, Italian varieties, and some Cabernet. Can derive from grapes, terroir (garrigue), or oak aging. Distinguished from herbaceous (greener) by suggesting dried herbs.

terms.short_definition: Wine lacking substance in the mid-palate.
terms.full_definition: Hollow describes wines that start with promising aromas but disappear in the mid-palate, lacking core fruit and substance. The attack and finish may be present, but the middle is empty. Usually indicates dilution from high yields or poor extraction. A significant structural flaw.

terms.short_definition: Distinctive characteristics consistent across a producer's wines.
terms.full_definition: House style refers to the consistent characteristics that define a producer's wines across vintages and cuvees. Particularly important in Champagne where blending creates recognizable signatures: Krug's richness, Salon's purity, Bollinger's power. House style provides continuity despite vintage variation.

terms.short_definition: Intensely sweet wine from naturally frozen grapes.
terms.full_definition: Ice wine is made from grapes naturally frozen on the vine and pressed while frozen. Water remains as ice, concentrating sugars and acids in the juice. German Eiswein and Canadian icewine are the benchmarks. Production is weather-dependent and risky. Wines are intensely sweet with balancing acidity and can age for decades.

terms.short_definition: Italian quality tier below DOC allowing more flexibility.
terms.full_definition: IGT (Indicazione Geografica Tipica) is an Italian wine classification below DOC/DOCG, offering winemakers more flexibility with grape varieties and techniques. Many 'Super Tuscan' wines used IGT to make non-traditional blends. France's equivalent is IGP (Indication Geographique Protegee). Now being phased into European IGP system.

terms.short_definition: Chemically unreactive or lacking expressive character.
terms.full_definition: Inert has two wine meanings: (1) in winemaking, refers to non-reactive vessels (stainless steel, glass, concrete) that don't impart flavor or oxygen; (2) in tasting, describes wines lacking expressiveness or energy. Inert vessels are valued for preserving pure fruit character without oak influence.

terms.short_definition: Fish bladder protein used to fine white wines.
terms.full_definition: Isinglass is a fining agent derived from fish swim bladders, traditionally used for white wines. The collagen protein binds with tannins and suspended particles, settling them out. Preferred for delicate whites where stronger agents might strip flavor. Not vegan; alternative agents include bentonite and pea protein.

terms.short_definition: Large wine bottle holding 3-4.5 liters.
terms.full_definition: A Jeroboam is a large format bottle, though size varies by region: in Champagne and Burgundy, it holds 3 liters (4 bottles); in Bordeaux, 4.5 liters (6 bottles). Large formats age more slowly and gracefully due to lower oxygen-to-wine ratio. Named after biblical king.

terms.short_definition: French for young vines, often bottled separately.
terms.full_definition: Jeunes vignes ('young vines') refers to vines typically under 15-20 years old. Their grapes often lack the concentration and complexity of old vines, so many producers bottle young vine fruit separately under this designation. The resulting wine is typically lighter and earlier-drinking than the estate's main wine.

terms.short_definition: Wine with fresh, succulent fruit character.
terms.full_definition: Juicy describes wines with fresh, succulent, mouth-watering fruit character. Often associated with good acidity and youthful vigor. Juicy wines are immediately appealing and drinkable. Common in Gamay, youthful Pinot Noir, and fruit-forward styles. The opposite of austere or dried out.

terms.short_definition: German quality level for light, elegant wines.
terms.full_definition: Kabinett is the first Pradikat level in German wine classification, indicating grapes with minimum must weight of 67-85 Oechsle (depending on region). Kabinett wines are typically light, fresh, and lower in alcohol, often with some residual sugar balanced by high acidity. The style originated in the 18th century cabinet cellars.

terms.short_definition: Vast wine region in southern France.
terms.full_definition: The Languedoc (formerly Languedoc-Roussillon) is France's largest wine region along the Mediterranean coast. Historically known for bulk production, quality has dramatically improved with many excellent value wines and ambitious appellations (Minervois, Corbieres, Pic Saint-Loup). Climate suits Grenache, Syrah, Mourvedre, and Carignan.

terms.short_definition: Vine disease causing reduced ripening and quality.
terms.full_definition: Leafroll virus is a widespread grapevine disease spread by mealybugs and infected propagation material. It reduces photosynthesis, delays ripening, decreases sugar accumulation, and affects color development. Infected vines produce lower quality grapes. No cure exists; prevention involves clean plant material and pest control.

terms.short_definition: Wine lacking generous fruit or body.
terms.full_definition: Lean describes wines lacking generous fruit and body, showing more structure than flesh. Can be positive (taut, fresh, mineral) or negative (thin, underfed) depending on style. Cool vintage wines, young wines needing time, or deliberately restrained styles can be positively lean. Context determines interpretation.

terms.short_definition: Calcium carbonate rock prized for wine terroir.
terms.full_definition: Limestone is a sedite rocks and its derived soils are associated with many fine wine regions: Burgundy, Champagne, the Loire, Chablis, and parts of Rioja. Limestone provides good drainage, moderate water retention, and alkaline conditions. Wines from limestone often show mineral character and vibrant acidity.

terms.short_definition: Brilliantly clear wine without cloudiness.
terms.full_definition: Limpid describes wines with perfect clarity, free from any haze or suspended particles. Indicates proper fining, filtration, and stability. While modern wines are typically limpid, natural and unfiltered wines may be hazy yet sound. Limpidity is assessed visually against light.

terms.short_definition: Extended flavor persistence after swallowing.
terms.full_definition: Long describes wines with extended flavor persistence, typically 10+ seconds after swallowing. Exceptionally long wines may persist 60+ seconds. Length is a key quality indicator, suggesting concentration and complexity. A short finish often indicates dilution or fault. Also measured as 'caudalies' (1 second each).

terms.short_definition: Waiting list for allocation to high-demand wines.
terms.full_definition: A mailing list is a customer database for wineries with demand exceeding supply. Members receive periodic allocation offers. For cult wines, lists can have multi-year waits. Members may need to purchase every release to maintain position. The allocation system creates artificial scarcity and prestige.

terms.short_definition: Grape pomace or brandy distilled from it.
terms.full_definition: Marc has two meanings: (1) the grape pomace (skins, seeds, stems) remaining after pressing; (2) the brandy distilled from this pomace (French marc, Italian grappa). Pomace marc is sometimes used for compost, distillation, or extraction of tartrates. Marc brandies can be rustic or refined depending on production method.

terms.short_definition: Wine that has developed with age and is ready to drink.
terms.full_definition: Mature describes wine that has aged sufficiently to develop complexity, integrate components, and reach optimal drinking. Primary fruit evolves into secondary and tertiary characteristics. Determining maturity is subjective; some prefer youthful wines while others wait decades. Most wine is made for immediate consumption.

terms.short_definition: Traditional bottle fermentation method for sparkling wine.
terms.full_definition: Methode traditionnelle (traditional method, Champagne method outside Champagne) is sparkling wine production through secondary fermentation in bottle. Wine is bottled with yeast and sugar, ferments, ages on lees, is riddled, disgorged, and given dosage. Used for Champagne, Cremant, Cava, Franciacorta, and quality sparkling worldwide.

terms.short_definition: The middle portion of the tasting experience.
terms.full_definition: Mid-palate refers to flavors and textures experienced in the middle of the tasting progression, between the initial attack and the finish. A full, satisfying mid-palate indicates concentration and quality. Hollow or thin mid-palate suggests dilution or poor extraction. Critical for assessing wine structure.

terms.short_definition: Uneven berry development from poor pollination.
terms.full_definition: Millerandage (hen and chicks, shot berries) results from poor pollination, causing grapes in a cluster to develop unevenly with some remaining small and seedless. Weather during flowering is usually the cause. Can reduce yields but seedless berries may increase concentration. Common in cool climates and with certain varieties.

terms.short_definition: Non-fruit, stony, or chalky character suggesting terroir.
terms.full_definition: Mineral describes non-fruit flavors suggesting stones, chalk, wet rocks, flint, or oyster shell. Associated with terroir expression, especially limestone and slate soils. The science of how soil minerals transfer to wine flavor remains debated. Chablis, Mosel Riesling, and Loire wines often show mineral character.

terms.short_definition: Stale, moldy aroma from contamination or poor storage.
terms.full_definition: Musty describes stale, moldy, damp basement aromas indicating wine contamination or poor storage. Often caused by dirty barrels, cork taint, or cellar mold. A fault that overpowers wine character. Distinguished from positive earthy notes by its unpleasant, stale quality.

terms.short_definition: Wild yeast found naturally in vineyard or winery.
terms.full_definition: Native yeast (wild, indigenous, ambient) refers to Saccharomyces and other yeasts naturally present in the vineyard or winery environment. Native yeast fermentations may start slowly, proceed unpredictably, and produce more complex wines with terroir expression. Risk of stuck fermentation is higher than with cultured yeast.

terms.short_definition: Noble Italian grape producing Barolo and Barbaresco.
terms.full_definition: Nebbiolo is the great red grape of Piedmont, Italy, producing powerful, complex wines in Barolo and Barbaresco. Named possibly for the fog (nebbia) during harvest. The wines show pale garnet color but robust tannins, with tar, roses, dried cherry, and truffle aromas. Requires significant aging. Rarely successful outside Piedmont.

terms.short_definition: Wine with tense, vibrant, high-strung acidity.
terms.full_definition: Nervous describes wines with tense, vibrant, high-strung acidity that creates energy and tension. Neither positive nor negative necessarily; context determines meaning. Champagne and cool climate whites often show nervosity. Can indicate youth, cool vintage, or wine style. Related to French 'nerveux.'

terms.short_definition: Lacking distinctive character; or non-reactive vessel.
terms.full_definition: Neutral has two wine meanings: (1) tasting term for wines lacking distinctive character or varietal expression; (2) winemaking term for non-reactive vessels (stainless steel, old oak) that don't impart flavor. Neutral grapes (Ugni Blanc) are used for distillation. Neutral can be fault or intentional style.

terms.short_definition: Wine blended from multiple years for consistency.
terms.full_definition: Non-vintage (NV) wine blends multiple harvest years to achieve consistent house style, most associated with Champagne. Reserves from previous years add complexity and buffer vintage variation. NV doesn't mean lower quality; it's a philosophical choice. Most Champagne production is NV.

terms.short_definition: Aromas suggesting nuts, often from oxidation or age.
terms.full_definition: Nutty describes aromas of hazelnut, almond, walnut, or cashew. Can result from controlled oxidation (Sherry, aged Champagne), lees aging, or bottle development. Appropriate in Sherry, aged white Burgundy, and vin jaune. In young wine, may indicate premature oxidation fault.

terms.short_definition: Small oak pieces used as cheaper alternative to barrels.
terms.full_definition: Oak chips are small pieces of oak added to wine in tank as a cost-effective alternative to barrel aging. They impart oak flavors (vanilla, toast, spice) much faster than barrels but without the micro-oxygenation benefits. Common for value wines. Also available as staves, spirals, and powder.

terms.short_definition: Lavishly rich and luxurious wine.
terms.full_definition: Opulent describes wines with lavish richness, generous fruit, and luxurious texture. Suggests abundance without excess. Opulent wines feel decadent and hedonistic. Common descriptor for top Burgundy, ripe Napa Cabernet, and premium dessert wines. The opposite of austere or restrained.

terms.short_definition: French term for topping up barrels to prevent oxidation.
terms.full_definition: Ouillage is the French term for topping up barrels to replace wine lost to evaporation (the 'angel's share'). Keeping barrels full minimizes oxygen exposure and prevents spoilage. Typically performed weekly to monthly. Some styles (Sherry, vin jaune) intentionally skip ouillage for oxidative character.

terms.short_definition: Vines producing excessive yields, diluting quality.
terms.full_definition: Over-cropped vines carry too many grape clusters, diluting sugars, flavors, and phenolics as the vine struggles to ripen excessive fruit. Results in thin, dilute wines lacking concentration. Yield limits in quality appellations prevent over-cropping. Green harvest removes excess clusters mid-season.

terms.short_definition: Wine with excessive tannin from aggressive extraction.
terms.full_definition: Over-extracted wines have harsh, bitter tannins from excessive maceration, pressing, or punch-downs. The wine may taste aggressive, astringent, and unbalanced. Common in wines pursuing concentration without finesse. Unlike firm young tannins that soften, over-extracted character often doesn't improve with age.

terms.short_definition: Spicy character reminiscent of black or white pepper.
terms.full_definition: Peppery describes spicy aromas and flavors of black or white pepper, common in Syrah/Shiraz, Gruner Veltliner, and some Zinfandels. Caused by rotundone compound. Can also describe the burning sensation from high alcohol. Peppery character is typically varietal and positive.

terms.short_definition: Intensely fragrant and aromatic wine.
terms.full_definition: Perfumed describes wines with intense, complex, lifted aromatics reminiscent of perfume. High praise suggesting sophisticated aromatic complexity. Often applied to Burgundy, aged Barolo, aromatic whites like Viognier, and fine aged wines. The opposite of muted or closed.

terms.short_definition: Petillant Naturel - sparkling wine bottled during fermentation.
terms.full_definition: Pet-nat (petillant naturel, methode ancestrale) is sparkling wine bottled before primary fermentation completes, trapping CO2 naturally. No secondary fermentation or dosage added. Results vary from lightly fizzy to fully sparkling, often cloudy with yeast. Popular in natural wine circles. Ancient method predating Champagne.

terms.short_definition: Full maturity of grape flavors beyond sugar ripeness.
terms.full_definition: Physiological (phenolic) ripeness occurs when grape skins, seeds, and pulp reach full flavor maturity, beyond simple sugar accumulation. Signs include brown seeds, easily detached stems, and ripe tannins. Harvesting at physiological ripeness prevents green, harsh character even in high-sugar fruit.

terms.short_definition: Traditional Port barrel holding about 550 liters.
terms.full_definition: A pipe is the traditional barrel size for Port wine, holding approximately 550 liters (534L for Port, 522L for Madeira). Port production and sales have historically been measured in pipes. The size allows extended aging. Half-pipes and quarter casks also exist.

terms.short_definition: Informal term for cheap, low-quality wine.
terms.full_definition: Plonk is British/Australian slang for cheap, undistinguished wine. Originally World War I slang from French 'vin blanc.' Used affectionately or dismissively depending on context. Not a technical term but widely understood to mean basic, inexpensive wine without pretension.

terms.short_definition: Rich, ripe plum fruit character.
terms.full_definition: Plummy describes wines with rich, ripe plum flavors, both fresh and dried. Common in Merlot, warm-climate Cabernet, and some Zinfandels. Often accompanies soft, approachable texture. Very ripe examples may show prune-like dried plum character.

terms.short_definition: Soft, velvety, luxurious texture.
terms.full_definition: Plush describes wines with soft, velvety, cushioned texture, like plush fabric. Suggests generous fruit, ripe tannins, and overall smoothness. A positive descriptor for wines aiming at approachability and comfort rather than austerity. Common for modern-style reds.

terms.short_definition: Refined wine with smooth, seamless character.
terms.full_definition: Polished describes refined wines with smooth, seamless integration of components. Nothing juts out; edges are rounded. Polished wines show careful winemaking and often fining/filtration. Can imply sophistication or, negatively, over-manipulation removing character. Context determines interpretation.

terms.short_definition: Heavy, sluggish wine lacking energy.
terms.full_definition: Ponderous describes heavy, sluggish wines that feel laborious to drink. Usually negative, suggesting excessive weight without compensating acidity or energy. Ponderous wines tire the palate quickly. Often results from overripe fruit, high alcohol, or low acidity.

terms.short_definition: Powerful wine, often high in alcohol.
terms.full_definition: Potent describes wines with power and strength, usually referring to high alcohol (14%+) creating a strong impact. Can be positive (powerful, concentrated) or cautionary (alcoholic, unbalanced). Potent wines demand attention and can overwhelm food pairings.

terms.short_definition: French term for noble rot (Botrytis cinerea).
terms.full_definition: Pourriture noble is the French term for noble rot, the beneficial form of Botrytis cinerea fungus that concentrates grape sugars and adds honeyed complexity. Essential for Sauternes, Barsac, and Loire sweet wines. Requires specific climate conditions: humid mornings, dry afternoons.

terms.short_definition: Wine with intense concentration and impact.
terms.full_definition: Powerful describes wines with great intensity, concentration, and impact. Powerful wines demand attention through deep flavors, firm structure, and often higher alcohol. Can be elegantly powerful (Barolo, Grand Cru Burgundy) or brashly powerful (young Napa Cabernet). The opposite of delicate or subtle.

terms.short_definition: German quality designation based on grape ripeness.
terms.full_definition: Pradikat is the German quality tier for wines classified by grape must weight (ripeness) at harvest. In ascending order: Kabinett, Spatlese, Auslese, Beerenauslese, Trockenbeerenauslese, and Eiswein. Each requires minimum Oechsle levels. The system emphasizes natural grape ripeness over winemaking intervention.

terms.short_definition: Wine showing clear, focused, well-defined character.
terms.full_definition: Precise describes wines with clear, focused, well-defined character where every element is sharply delineated. Precise wines show purposeful winemaking without vagueness or muddled flavors. Associated with cool climates, quality producers, and careful technique. High praise suggesting intention and execution aligned.

terms.short_definition: Marketing term suggesting higher quality, no legal definition.
terms.full_definition: Private Reserve is a marketing term used on wine labels implying superior quality, but it has no legal definition in most countries. It may indicate barrel selection, special lots, or simply marketing. Some wineries use it meaningfully; others arbitrarily. Buyer should research specific producers' practices.

terms.short_definition: Alcohol measurement system, double the ABV percentage.
terms.full_definition: Proof is an alcohol measurement where US proof equals double the ABV percentage (80 proof = 40% ABV). Historically from testing gunpowder ignition with spirits. Rarely used for wine but applies to fortified wines and spirits. UK formerly used different proof system.

terms.short_definition: Circulating fermenting wine over grape cap for extraction.
terms.full_definition: Pumping over (remontage) extracts color and tannin by pumping fermenting wine from the bottom of the tank and spraying it over the floating grape cap. More aggressive than punch-downs, it increases extraction and aeration. Frequency and duration affect wine style.

terms.short_definition: Sharp, penetrating aroma or flavor.
terms.full_definition: Pungent describes sharp, penetrating aromas that strongly impact the nose. Can be positive (intense aromatics) or negative (volatile acidity, sulfur). Pungent wines make an immediate impression. Common with aromatic varieties, high VA wines, or young wines with strong varietal character.

terms.short_definition: Clean, unadorned expression of fruit and terroir.
terms.full_definition: Purity describes wines showing clean, clear, unadorned expression of fruit and place. Pure wines don't hide behind oak or manipulation; they transparently reveal their origins. High praise suggesting authenticity and quality. Associated with minimal intervention and terroir-focused winemaking.

terms.short_definition: German quality wine from specified regions.
terms.full_definition: QbA (Qualitatswein bestimmter Anbaugebiete) is German quality wine from designated regions, below Pradikat level. May be chaptalized (sugar-added before fermentation). Must meet regional requirements for grape varieties and ripeness. The basic quality tier for German wines, above table wine but below Pradikat.

terms.short_definition: Easy-drinking wine meant for casual consumption.
terms.full_definition: Quaffable describes wines that go down easily, meant for drinking rather than analyzing. Quaffable wines are typically light, fresh, low in tannin, and immediately enjoyable. A positive term for everyday wines, though not high praise for ambitious bottlings. Perfect for parties and casual meals.

terms.short_definition: German quality wine designation.
terms.full_definition: Qualitatswein is the German term for quality wine, encompassing QbA and Pradikat levels. Must come from one of 13 designated wine regions, use approved grape varieties, and meet minimum ripeness levels. The term indicates official quality status above basic Deutscher Wein (table wine).

terms.short_definition: Portuguese wine estate or farm.
terms.full_definition: Quinta is the Portuguese term for a wine estate, farm, or country property. Prominent in Port production where many quintas produce single-estate wines. The term appears on many Portuguese wine labels indicating estate-produced wine. Similar to French domaine or chateau.

terms.short_definition: Georgian clay vessel for fermenting and aging wine.
terms.full_definition: Qvevri (also kvevri) are large clay vessels used in Georgia for fermenting and aging wine, buried underground for temperature control. The 8,000-year-old tradition involves fermenting white grapes on skins for months, creating amber/orange wines. UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage. Increasingly used by natural winemakers worldwide.

terms.short_definition: Dried grape character from overripe or dried fruit.
terms.full_definition: Raisiny describes flavors of dried grapes, suggesting overripeness, desiccation, or intentionally dried grape production (Amarone, Vin Santo). Can be positive in appropriate styles or negative in wines meant to show fresh fruit. Often accompanied by high alcohol and residual sugar.

terms.short_definition: Young, undeveloped wine with rough edges.
terms.full_definition: Raw describes young, undeveloped wines showing rough, unintegrated character. Raw tannins feel harsh; raw acidity seems sharp. Not necessarily a fault, as many fine wines are raw when young and need time to smooth out. Context and potential determine whether raw is temporary or problematic.

terms.short_definition: French term for pumping over during fermentation.
terms.full_definition: Remontage is the French term for pumping over: circulating fermenting wine from the bottom of the tank over the floating grape cap. More vigorous than pigeage (punch-downs), it increases extraction and aeration. Common for structured red wines needing color and tannin development.

terms.short_definition: Spanish/Portuguese designation for wines with extended aging.
terms.full_definition: Reserva is a legally defined term in Spain and Portugal indicating extended aging. Spanish red Reserva requires 3 years total aging with at least 1 year in oak. Gran Reserva requires 5 years. Portugal uses similar requirements. In other countries, 'Reserve' has no legal meaning.

terms.short_definition: Full, generous wine with ample fruit and texture.
terms.full_definition: Rich describes full, generous wines with ample fruit, body, and texture. Rich wines feel satisfying and complete. Distinguished from fat or heavy by maintaining balance. Richness can come from ripe fruit, oak aging, lees contact, or residual sugar. Generally positive when balanced.

terms.short_definition: Full-bodied, sturdy wine with strong character.
terms.full_definition: Robust describes full-bodied, sturdy wines with assertive character, firm structure, and substantial presence. Robust wines feel solid and powerful without being clumsy. Often applied to hearty reds suited to rich foods. The opposite of delicate or ethereal.

terms.short_definition: Simple, rough wine with earthy, unpolished character.
terms.full_definition: Rustic describes wines with earthy, unpolished, rough-edged character suggesting traditional or peasant winemaking. Can be positive (authentic, characterful, unpretentious) or negative (crude, unrefined). Rustic wines often pair well with hearty, country-style foods. The opposite of polished or refined.

terms.short_definition: French for 'without sulfur' - wine made without added sulfites.
terms.full_definition: Sans soufre (without sulfur) indicates wine made with no added sulfur dioxide. Part of the natural wine movement, these wines rely on careful winemaking and cool storage for stability. Small amounts of sulfur occur naturally during fermentation. Sans soufre wines can be fragile and variable.

terms.short_definition: French term for dry, though meaning varies by context.
terms.full_definition: Sec means dry in French but has different meanings in different contexts. For still wine, sec means dry. For Champagne, sec means off-dry (17-32 g/L sugar), drier than demi-sec but sweeter than brut. Context determines interpretation.

terms.short_definition: Estate's secondary label from younger vines or declassified lots.
terms.full_definition: Second wine is a chateau or estate's secondary bottling, typically from younger vines, lighter lots, or barrels not selected for the grand vin. Offers similar style at lower price. Famous examples include Carruades de Lafite, Pavillon Rouge, and Clarence de Haut-Brion.

terms.short_definition: Wine with noticeable but not dominant sweetness.
terms.full_definition: Semi-sweet wines have noticeable sweetness (typically 20-70 g/L RS) but aren't fully dessert wines. The term is imprecise and used differently in different regions. German wines use amabile, moelleux, or halbtrocken. Sweetness often balances high acidity.

terms.short_definition: Wine with brief finish lacking persistence.
terms.full_definition: Short describes wines whose flavors disappear quickly after swallowing, typically under 5 seconds. Short finish often indicates dilution, low quality, or young wine not yet expressing potential. Length is a key quality indicator; great wines have long finishes.

terms.short_definition: Wine lacking complexity or depth.
terms.full_definition: Simple describes wines with limited complexity, showing one or two dimensions rather than layers. Not necessarily negative: simple wines can be pleasant, refreshing, and appropriate for casual drinking. But simple is critique for ambitious or expensive wines expected to show depth.

terms.short_definition: Aromas of smoke, often from oak or terroir.
terms.full_definition: Smoky describes aromas suggesting smoke, wood fire, or char. Can derive from toasted oak barrels, certain terroirs (northern Rhone), or actual smoke exposure from wildfires. Subtle smokiness adds complexity; excessive smokiness from fire damage is a fault (smoke taint).

terms.short_definition: Complex, refined wine showing finesse and nuance.
terms.full_definition: Sophisticated describes wines showing complexity, finesse, and nuance beyond simple fruit. Sophisticated wines reward attention and suggest intelligent winemaking. The term implies refinement and elegance rather than power. High praise for ambitious wines.

terms.short_definition: French term for racking wine off sediment.
terms.full_definition: Soutirage is the French term for racking, transferring wine from one vessel to another to separate it from sediment. A key step in elevage, affecting clarity and oxygen exposure. Timing and frequency depend on wine style goals.

terms.short_definition: Wine with significant effervescence from dissolved CO2.
terms.full_definition: Sparkling wine contains dissolved carbon dioxide creating bubbles. Made by traditional method (Champagne), tank method (Prosecco), carbonation, or ancestral method (pet-nat). Pressure typically 5-6 atmospheres. Categories include Champagne, Cava, Cremant, Franciacorta, and many others.

terms.short_definition: Slight effervescence or prickle in wine.
terms.full_definition: Spritz describes slight effervescence, a mild prickle of CO2 on the tongue. Intentional in frizzante wines like Vinho Verde. Unintentional spritz in still wine may indicate refermentation (fault) or simply CO2 retained from fermentation. Context determines whether spritz is welcome or problematic.

terms.short_definition: Green, stemmy character from grape stems.
terms.full_definition: Stalky describes green, vegetal, bitter flavors extracted from grape stems during fermentation. Whole-cluster fermentation intentionally includes stems for structure and spice; excessive stalky character suggests unripe stems or over-extraction. Can be positive in small amounts, negative in excess.

terms.short_definition: Crisp, mineral, high-acid character suggesting steel.
terms.full_definition: Steely describes crisp, austere wines with firm acidity and mineral character, suggesting the metallic brightness of steel. Often applied to Chablis, unoaked Riesling, and cool-climate whites. Steely wines feel taut and precise. A compliment for wines where freshness and tension are prized.

terms.short_definition: Juicy, mouth-watering wine with fresh fruit.
terms.full_definition: Succulent describes wines that are juicy, mouth-watering, and alive with fresh fruit character. Succulent wines make you want another sip. The term suggests ripeness and freshness in harmony. Common for fruit-forward wines with good acidity maintaining freshness.

terms.short_definition: Soft, flexible wine with yielding texture.
terms.full_definition: Supple describes wines with soft, yielding, flexible texture. Supple tannins bend rather than grip. The term suggests elegance and drinkability. Supple wines are approachable and enjoyable young. Often develops with bottle age as tannins polymerize.

terms.short_definition: RM - Champagne grower who makes wine from own grapes.
terms.full_definition: Recoltant Manipulant (RM) designates a Champagne grower who grows grapes and produces wine entirely from their own vineyards. Shown as 'RM' on labels, it distinguishes grower Champagne from negociant houses (NM). RM producers often offer terroir-focused, artisanal Champagnes at value prices.

terms.short_definition: NM - Champagne house buying grapes from growers.
terms.full_definition: Negociant Manipulant (NM) designates a Champagne producer who buys grapes or must from growers rather than relying solely on estate vineyards. Most major Champagne houses (Moet, Veuve Clicquot, Krug) are NM. They may own vineyards but also purchase widely to maintain volume and style.

terms.short_definition: CM - Champagne cooperative producing for member growers.
terms.full_definition: Cooperative Manipulant (CM) designates Champagne made by a cooperative from members' grapes. The cooperative vinifies and may sell under its own label or return wine to members to sell under their names. Quality varies; some cooperatives produce excellent wines.

terms.short_definition: Traditional shallow silver wine tasting cup.
terms.full_definition: A tastevin is a small, shallow silver cup traditionally used for tasting wine in Burgundy's dark cellars. The dimpled surface and shiny metal helped evaluate color and clarity in low light. Now largely ceremonial; the Confrerie des Chevaliers du Tastevin is Burgundy's famous wine brotherhood.

terms.short_definition: Oxidatively aged Port with amber color.
terms.full_definition: Tawny Port is aged in small barrels, exposing it to oxygen and creating amber/tawny color and nutty, caramel flavors. Categories include basic Tawny, Tawny with Age Indication (10, 20, 30, 40 year), and Colheita (single vintage). Served slightly chilled, unlike Ruby Port.

terms.short_definition: Droplets running down glass after swirling wine.
terms.full_definition: Tears (legs, curtains) are droplets that form and run down the inside of a glass after swirling. Caused by the Marangoni effect from alcohol evaporation creating surface tension differences. Slow, thick tears indicate higher alcohol, not quality. A visual phenomenon, not taste indicator.

terms.short_definition: Closed, restrained wine not showing full potential.
terms.full_definition: Tight describes wines that feel restrained, closed, and not yet expressing their full potential. Flavors and aromas seem compact and unrevealing. Young wines are often tight, needing time or aeration to open. Tight structure can indicate aging potential. The opposite of open or expressive.

terms.short_definition: French term for barrel or cask; also a volume measure.
terms.full_definition: Tonneau is French for barrel/cask, and also a traditional Bordeaux volume measure equaling 900 liters (4 barriques/100 cases). Bordeaux prices were historically quoted per tonneau. The term tonnellerie refers to the barrel-making craft and cooperage business.

terms.short_definition: The first, most volatile aromas detected.
terms.full_definition: Top notes are the first, most volatile aromas perceived when smelling wine, including alcohol, esters, and light aromatics. They're the 'first impression' aromas that fade quickly, revealing deeper heart and base notes. Important for initial wine evaluation.

terms.short_definition: Sparkling wine bottle-fermented then filtered and rebottled.
terms.full_definition: Transfer method involves bottle fermentation like traditional method, but instead of riddling and disgorging each bottle, wine is transferred to a tank for filtration, then rebottled under pressure. Faster than traditional method but less prestigious. Avoids hand riddling costs.

terms.short_definition: German dessert wine from individually selected dried berries.
terms.full_definition: Trockenbeerenauslese (TBA) is the highest Pradikat level, made from individually picked, botrytis-shriveled berries. Requires minimum 150+ Oechsle. Intensely sweet, honeyed wines with extraordinary concentration and longevity. Extremely rare and expensive; production depends entirely on weather conditions.

terms.short_definition: French term for harvest or grape picking.
terms.full_definition: Vendange is the French term for grape harvest. Vendanges (plural) refers to the harvest season. Vendange Tardive means late harvest (used in Alsace for sweet wines). Vendange Verte (green harvest) is the summer practice of dropping unripe clusters to concentrate remaining fruit.

terms.short_definition: Strong vine growth; or energetic wine character.
terms.full_definition: Vigorous has two meanings: (1) in viticulture, describes vines with strong vegetative growth requiring management to balance fruit and leaves; (2) in tasting, describes wines with energetic, lively, forceful character suggesting vitality and youth. Context determines specific meaning.

terms.short_definition: Wine from a specific commune, below Premier Cru level.
terms.full_definition: Village wines come from a named commune/village but not from classified vineyard sites. In Burgundy, Village level is below Premier Cru and Grand Cru but above regional appellation. Village wines can include estate fruit not deemed worthy of cru designation or blend from multiple village plots.

terms.short_definition: Oxidative yellow wine from France's Jura region.
terms.full_definition: Vin Jaune (yellow wine) from the Jura is made from Savagnin grapes aged under a film of flor yeast for minimum 6 years and 3 months without topping up. The oxidative aging creates unique nutty, curry-like character. Bottled in 620ml clavelins. Similar to dry Sherry but unfortified.

terms.short_definition: Prestigious Sangiovese wine from Montepulciano, Tuscany.
terms.full_definition: Vino Nobile di Montepulciano is a DOCG red wine from the Tuscan town of Montepulciano, made primarily from Sangiovese (locally called Prugnolo Gentile). First wine to receive DOCG status (1980). Not to be confused with Montepulciano d'Abruzzo, a different grape variety.

terms.short_definition: Wine region with high temperatures during growing season.
terms.full_definition: Warm climate wine regions have high temperatures during the growing season, producing ripe, full-bodied wines with lower acidity and higher alcohol. Examples include Napa Valley, Barossa, southern Rhone, and much of Spain. Warm climate wines tend toward fruit-forward styles. Contrasts with cool climate.

terms.short_definition: Fermenting grapes with stems intact.
terms.full_definition: Whole bunch (whole cluster) fermentation includes grape stems in the ferment. Stems contribute tannin structure, spice, and complexity when ripe. Unripe stems add harsh, green character. Burgundy and Rhone producers use varying percentages. Requires careful stem ripeness assessment.

terms.short_definition: European surplus wine from overproduction subsidies.
terms.full_definition: Wine Lake refers to the massive wine surplus that accumulated in the EU from agricultural subsidies encouraging production regardless of demand. Surplus wine was distilled into industrial alcohol. EU reforms in the 2000s reduced the wine lake through vineyard grubbing schemes and market reform.

terms.short_definition: Facility where wine is produced.
terms.full_definition: A winery is the facility where grapes are crushed, fermented, aged, and bottled into wine. May range from small garages to vast industrial complexes. May or may not include vineyards (estate vs. custom crush). Regional terms include domaine, chateau, bodega, cantina, and weingut.

terms.short_definition: Tannin extracted from oak barrels.
terms.full_definition: Wood tannins (ellagitannins) are extracted from oak during barrel aging, adding to grape tannins (condensed tannins). Wood tannins contribute drier, more aggressive character than grape tannins. New oak imparts more wood tannin. Over time, wood tannins integrate with wine structure.
